recent: Iranian crude exports oscillated between ~1.4 and ~2.1 mb/d across mid-March, averaging ~1.7 mb/d — well below pre-war ~2.5 mb/d but still flowing as Iran controls the Strait. The WoW volatility (2.1 mb/d in 9–15 Mar → 1.5 in 16–22 Mar → 2.0 in 23–29 Mar → 1.4 in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr) reflects intermittent Kharg Island loading windows and IRGC-managed convoy scheduling.

China surfaced as the only visible destination in 23–29 Mar at ~148 kbd, though total exports of ~2.0 mb/d suggest the bulk moves via ship-to-ship transfers or to undisclosed Asian buyers. Trump's 48-hr ultimatum (Apr 6 deadline) and the Mahshahr petchem zone strikes may further depress loading capacity in the coming days.

Iran's ability to sustain ~1.5–2.0 mb/d while the Strait remains closed to all non-Iranian traffic underscores the asymmetry of the blockade — Iran continues to monetize its crude while competitors' exports collapse.
quarterly: Iran's crude exports proved remarkably resilient through Q1 2026, averaging ~1.7 mb/d across the quarter with no meaningful decline post-Hormuz closure — Kharg Island sits outside the Strait, allowing continued loadings even as Iran's IRGC blockaded transit for everyone else.

China remains effectively the sole buyer, absorbing almost all tracked volumes. A mid-February spike to 3.8 mb/d (16–22 Feb) likely reflected pre-conflict stockpiling, but even in late March exports held at ~1.5–2.0 mb/d — consistent with S&P Global reports of Kharg loading ~1.5 mb/d.

Destination visibility deteriorated sharply after the war began — several March weeks show no named buyers despite 1.5–2.0 mb/d in loadings, suggesting cargoes to smaller or sanctioned intermediaries. The IRGC's toll-charging ($2M/vessel in yuan per NBC News) creates an asymmetric advantage: Iran exports freely while competitors cannot.
yearly: Iran's crude exports averaged ~1.75 mb/d through 2025, nearly all to China via dark fleet, with a brief spike to 2.17 mb/d in February 2026 likely reflecting pre-conflict front-loading. March saw headline volumes hold at 1.83 mb/d, but tracked China-bound flows collapsed to just 182 kbd/d (from 625 kbd/d in Feb) -- the gap indicating an expanding shadow fleet operating outside Kpler visibility.

As a combatant state, Iran's Kharg Island loadings continue at ~1.5 mb/d per S&P Global, but Tehran's customer base has narrowed to effectively one buyer (China) with zero diversification options. The April partial (1.45 mb/d, zero identified destinations) underscores the opacity of sanctioned flows during wartime.

Structurally, Iran's 2025 export range of 1.4-1.9 mb/d remained remarkably stable despite the escalation -- the same sanctions-evasion infrastructure that circumvented US/EU restrictions now provides wartime resilience that its Gulf neighbours lack.

recent: Iranian naphtha exports to the UAE ran at ~46 kbd in 9–22 Mar (likely supplying Ruwais or ENOC blending), then fell to zero from 23 Mar onward. The halt coincides with UAE refinery shutdowns — Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Habshan gas facility struck again on Apr 3 — eliminating the primary intra-Gulf buyer.

With Asian petchem crackers (FPCC, YNCC, Chandra Asri) all under FM due to naphtha shortages, Iran's inability to redirect naphtha outside the Gulf represents a secondary casualty of the conflict geography.
quarterly: Iran's naphtha exports collapsed from a pre-crisis average of ~175 kbd (nearly all flowing to UAE blending operations in Fujairah) to effectively zero by late March. The 9–15 Mar and 16–22 Mar weeks showed residual volumes of ~46 kbd to UAE, but the trade is functionally dead.

This collapse reflects both Hormuz transit restrictions and the broader shutdown of UAE refining/blending operations (Ruwais offline, Fujairah bunkering hub under FM), eliminating Iran's primary naphtha customer.
yearly: Iran's naphtha exports collapsed from a 2025 average of ~180 kbd (overwhelmingly shipped to the UAE for blending and re-export) to just 23.5 kbd in March 2026 -- an 87% drop. The Hormuz closure severed what was almost entirely an intra-Gulf trade route, with UAE deliveries falling from 122 kbd/d in February to 17 kbd/d in March and zero in early April.

This disruption cascades directly into UAE and regional petrochemical supply chains: Iranian naphtha was a key feedstock for Ruwais and Jebel Ali crackers, compounding the naphtha shortage that has pushed global naphtha prices past $1,000/mt and triggered FM declarations from Formosa, YNCC, and Chandra Asri.

recent: Iraq's crude exports recovered from near-zero (~30 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to a stable ~250 kbd by 23–29 Mar, running at the same 250 kbd pace in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr — entirely via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey. This represents just 7.6% of Iraq's pre-war 3.3 mb/d export capacity.

The Basra maritime route remains completely shut following the Iraq Oil Ministry's FM on all foreign-operated oilfields (Mar 20). SOMO's Syria overland route (~50 kbd crude + 650k mt/month fuel oil via Baniyas) likely adds another trickle not visible in seaborne data. Iran's Apr 4 declaration exempting Iraqi ships from Hormuz restrictions could theoretically reopen Basra, but tanker nominations remain impossible amid active hostilities.

At current Kirkuk-Ceyhan throughput, Iraq is losing ~$700M+ per week in crude revenue versus pre-war levels — a fiscal catastrophe that will force difficult decisions on post-war reconstruction financing.
quarterly: Iraq's crude exports suffered the most dramatic collapse of any Gulf exporter — from a pre-crisis average of ~3.3 mb/d (Jan–late Feb) to effectively zero via maritime routes. The Basra FM declaration on 20 Mar formalized what was already happening: tanker nominations became impossible once Hormuz closed.

The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey is now Iraq's sole functioning export route, running at a steady 250 kbd from mid-March onwards — just 7% of pre-war capacity. Per The National, Iraq is also routing ~50 kbd crude plus 650k mt/month fuel oil overland through Syria to Baniyas, but these volumes barely register.

The customer base inverted entirely: pre-crisis, India (1.0 mb/d) and China (1.1 mb/d) dominated alongside European and Asian buyers. Post-crisis, Turkey is the only destination. Iraq plans to boost Kirkuk-Ceyhan to 650 kbd, but this would still leave 80% of pre-war exports stranded.
yearly: Iraq's crude exports suffered the most dramatic structural collapse of any Gulf exporter: from a 2025 baseline of 3.2-3.4 mb/d (to 15+ destinations across Asia, Europe, and the Americas) to just 683 kbd in March 2026 -- an 80% reduction. The only meaningful outlet is the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline through Turkey, which carried ~121 kbd/d to Ceyhan in March (ramped from zero pre-crisis), plus residual loadings to China (219 kbd/d) and India (66 kbd/d) from stockpiled cargoes.

The customer base collapsed from 14 countries in February to just 6 in March, with the US, South Korea, Japan, and all European buyers vanishing entirely. Per Fortune, Iran declared Iraqi ships exempt from Hormuz restrictions on April 4, but this has not yet materialized in flow data -- early April shows only Turkey (250 kbd/d via Kirkuk-Ceyhan) with zero seaborne exports.

Iraq's Oil Ministry FM on all foreign-operated oilfields with Basra output at 900 kbd (from 3.3M pre-war) means even if Hormuz reopened tomorrow, the production restart timeline would be measured in months. The SOMO Syria route (50 kbd crude + fuel oil overland to Baniyas) provides token volumes but cannot replace Basra's deep-water loading capacity.

recent: Iraqi naphtha exports were negligible at ~13–15 kbd, appearing sporadically (W11, W13, W14) with a small cargo to the UAE in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. This is consistent with the near-total collapse of Basra refining operations and the FM declaration shutting down all major processing.
quarterly: Iraq's naphtha exports were negligible pre-crisis (averaging ~25 kbd when cargoes moved, with many zero weeks) and have effectively ceased. The Basra shutdown eliminated Iraq's primary naphtha source; a tiny 13 kbd cargo to UAE in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week is the only post-crisis activity.
yearly: Iraq's naphtha exports were already sporadic in 2025 (ranging 2-72 kbd monthly), and have effectively ceased in the crisis: March volumes fell to 6.3 kbd with no identified destinations. A small 20 kbd/d flow to the UAE appeared in early April, likely residual stock.
With Basra refineries offline and southern NGL processing halted, Iraq has no naphtha surplus to export -- the commodity was always a byproduct of refinery operations that are now shut.

recent: Kuwait crude exports have effectively ceased — zero in 3 of 4 weeks, with a single ~78 kbd blip in 16–22 Mar (likely one pre-loaded VLCC). Production has collapsed to ~500 kbd (81% below the 2.6 mb/d baseline) following 9+ direct Iranian attacks including the KPC Shuwaikh HQ set ablaze by drones on Apr 5.

The Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes (Mar 19–20) and KPC's FM declaration have eliminated Kuwait's entire export apparatus. With KPC estimating 3–4 months to restore full capacity post-war, Kuwait faces the longest recovery timeline among Gulf producers even after hostilities end.
quarterly: Kuwait's crude exports collapsed from ~1.1 mb/d average in Jan–early March to zero by mid-March — one of the most complete shutdowns among Gulf producers. The last meaningful export week was 2–8 Mar at 1.2 mb/d (to China); from 9 Mar onwards, volumes flatlined at 0–78 kbd.

Unlike Saudi Arabia and UAE, Kuwait has no pipeline bypass infrastructure. With the Al-Ahmadi refinery struck by drones on 19–20 Mar and KPC headquarters set ablaze on 5 Apr (Bloomberg), production has collapsed to ~500 kbd — 81% below the 2.6 mb/d baseline — with no export outlet available.

Pre-crisis, Kuwait served a diverse buyer base including India, Vietnam, China, S. Korea, Japan, Taiwan, and Myanmar. All of these customers have been forced to find alternative suppliers, primarily Russia and US Gulf Coast barrels.
yearly: Kuwait's crude exports collapsed from a 2025 average of ~1.35 mb/d to just 280 kbd in March 2026 -- an 81% drop and the steepest proportional decline among Gulf exporters, consistent with KPC's FM declaration and production falling to ~500 kbd (from 2.6M baseline). Unlike Saudi Arabia and UAE, Kuwait has no pipeline bypass infrastructure, leaving it entirely dependent on Hormuz-transiting tankers.

The customer base imploded from 10 countries in February (led by Vietnam, South Korea, China, and Japan) to a single buyer in March: China at 65 kbd/d. April shows zero exports -- total shutdown. The Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes of March 19-20 compounded the maritime blockade with physical infrastructure damage.

Kuwait is the Gulf's most structurally vulnerable exporter: no pipeline bypass, no non-Hormuz port, and now 9+ direct attacks including the KPC Shuwaikh HQ ablaze on April 5. KPC estimates 3-4 months to restore full capacity even after the conflict ends.

recent: Kuwait diesel exports collapsed from ~102 kbd in 9–15 Mar (split between Qatar and Djibouti — likely military/emergency supply) to zero in all subsequent weeks. The Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction has eliminated Kuwait's refined product processing capacity entirely.
The W11 shipment to Qatar (48 kbd) and Djibouti (54 kbd) were likely the last cargoes loaded before the refinery was struck, representing the final pre-attack inventory clearance.
quarterly: Kuwait's diesel/gasoil exports followed the crude collapse trajectory — from a pre-crisis average of ~265 kbd (serving European and African markets) to zero by 16 Mar. The last cargoes (102 kbd, 9–15 Mar) went to Djibouti and Qatar, the latter likely an emergency fuel transfer between Gulf neighbours.
With Al-Ahmadi refinery offline and no bypass route, Kuwait's entire refined products export chain has shut down. KPC estimates 3–4 months to restore capacity post-war.
yearly: Kuwait's gasoil/diesel exports averaged ~230 kbd through 2025, serving a diverse customer base spanning Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. March 2026 volumes cratered to 61 kbd (down 73%), with only Djibouti (22 kbd/d -- likely military/humanitarian), Qatar (11 kbd/d), and UAE as remaining destinations.

The pre-crisis customer mix was remarkably global (Germany, France, UK, South Africa, Pakistan, Netherlands), but the Hormuz closure and Al-Ahmadi refinery damage eliminated both the production base and the export route simultaneously. April shows zero exports.

recent: Kuwait gasoline exports fell from 64 kbd in 9–15 Mar (entirely to Saudi Arabia — likely an emergency intra-GCC supply arrangement) to zero for two weeks, before a small ~28 kbd cargo appeared in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. The W14 cargo may indicate limited refinery restart or stored inventory release, but remains marginal against pre-war export capacity.
quarterly: Kuwait's gasoline exports were already minimal pre-crisis (~10–34 kbd in sporadic weeks). One notable anomaly: 64 kbd shipped to Saudi Arabia in 9–15 Mar — likely an emergency mutual-supply arrangement as the crisis unfolded. The trade has since ceased entirely.
yearly: Kuwait's gasoline exports were minimal throughout 2025 (0-22 kbd/d, highly sporadic), reflecting limited refinery surplus for this product. The March 2026 figure of 20 kbd -- sent entirely to Saudi Arabia (448 kbd monthly) -- likely represents intra-Gulf emergency supply rather than commercial trade.
With Al-Ahmadi offline and production collapsed, Kuwait has shifted from a marginal gasoline exporter to a net importer requiring regional supply.

recent: Oman is the breakout story of the crisis — crude exports surged 150% from ~550 kbd (9–15 Mar) to ~1.4 mb/d in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr, as Oman's export terminals at Mina Al Fahal sit entirely outside the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint.

The customer base expanded rapidly: China-only in W11 (550 kbd), then Taiwan joined in W12, Japan in W13, and India + Singapore in the partial W14. This diversification signals desperate Asian buyers pivoting to the one Gulf producer that can reliably ship without Hormuz transit risk.

At Oman benchmark pricing of $152–153/bbl for May liftings — a $40+/bbl premium to pre-crisis levels — these volumes are generating windfall revenue. The trajectory suggests Oman may approach or exceed its pre-crisis ~950 kbd production capacity by leveraging inland fields at maximum output.
quarterly: Oman is the clear strategic winner of the Hormuz crisis. Crude exports actually increased across Q1 — from a Jan–Feb average of ~870 kbd to ~950 kbd in March, peaking at 1.37 mb/d in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week. Oman's export terminals sit outside the Strait, entirely bypassing the blockade.

China remains the overwhelmingly dominant buyer (~70–100% of weekly volumes), but new customers are appearing as buyers scramble for non-Hormuz barrels: India took 325 kbd in the latest partial week, Japan appeared at 269 kbd (23–29 Mar), and Singapore has become a regular lifter.

With the Oman benchmark surging to $152–153/bbl for May delivery (Zawya) and Omani-flagged vessels reportedly negotiating an Iran transit protocol, Oman is leveraging both its geographic advantage and diplomatic neutrality to maximize its crisis-era position.
yearly: Oman is the structural winner of the Hormuz crisis: crude exports averaged 940 kbd in March 2026, essentially flat against the 2025 average of ~960 kbd, because Oman's export terminals sit outside the Strait of Hormuz in the Gulf of Oman. Early April data (1,055 kbd/d in the first 4 days) actually shows a potential uptick as buyers scramble for accessible barrels.

China remains the dominant buyer at 757 kbd/d in March (81% share, consistent with 2025 patterns), but the crisis is reshaping Oman's customer mix: India surged to 488 kbd/d in early April (from near-zero in some 2025 months), and Japan appeared at 61 kbd/d in March -- both seeking non-Hormuz alternatives to replace lost Saudi/UAE supplies.

With Oman's benchmark trading at $152-153/bbl for May delivery (vs ~$70 a year ago), the sultanate is capturing unprecedented per-barrel margins. Three Omani-flagged vessels transited Hormuz on April 2 via an Iran-Oman transit protocol, suggesting Muscat may also serve as a transhipment hub.

recent: Oman LNG exports averaged ~32 ktons/d across 9–29 Mar, near the nameplate run-rate for its 10.4 Mtpa facility (~28.5 ktons/d) — with the facility outside Hormuz and commanding significant Asian spot premiums with Qatar's 77 Mtpa entirely offline.

India has emerged as the dominant buyer (21+ ktons/d in W12 and W13), consistent with India's urgent LNG procurement following Petronet LNG's FM declaration and Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cuts. The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr shows 24.3 ktons/d with India as the sole visible destination — Oman is now India's lifeline for LNG.

With Asian spot LNG at $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis forecast), Oman LNG is capturing extraordinary premiums on every cargo. Thailand, Japan, and Taiwan also competed for cargoes in W11–W12, underscoring the seller's market.
quarterly: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility — outside Hormuz — has maintained steady exports of ~30–43 ktons/day throughout Q1, completely unaffected by the crisis. With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline (~20% of global LNG supply), Oman is commanding significant Asian spot premiums per Platts LNG Daily.

Buyer diversification is notable: India, Japan, South Korea, Thailand, and Taiwan all receive regular cargoes. China appeared as a new buyer in mid-March (11 ktons/day, 16–22 Mar), likely redirecting from lost Qatari contracts.
yearly: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility -- positioned outside the Hormuz chokepoint -- has become a critical swing supplier with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline. March exports of 35.7 ktons/d were actually above the 2025 monthly average of ~30 ktons/d, demonstrating the plant is running at or near maximum capacity.

India emerged as the largest buyer in March at 16.8 ktons/d (47% of Oman's LNG exports), up from an average of ~5 ktons/d in 2025 -- a direct substitution for lost QatarEnergy volumes that supplied 41% of India's LNG imports. Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, and Thailand continue to compete for remaining cargoes.

Oman LNG is commanding significant Asian spot premiums per Platts LNG Daily, with JKM Asian spot at $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis forecast). However, at 10.4 Mtpa, Oman can only replace ~13% of Qatar's lost capacity -- the structural gap remains enormous.

recent: Oman jet fuel exports were sporadic — zero in W11, then 25 kbd to Djibouti in 16–22 Mar, doubling to 46 kbd to Djibouti in 23–29 Mar, before dropping back to zero in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. Djibouti's role as a major US/French military base hub in the Horn of Africa explains the destination — these are likely coalition refueling supply cargoes routed via Oman's non-Hormuz terminals.
quarterly: Oman's jet/kerosene exports are sporadic and low-volume, averaging ~50 kbd when cargoes move. Pre-crisis destinations included UK, Kenya, Mozambique, and Singapore; post-crisis, volumes shifted to Djibouti (25–46 kbd in late March), likely serving military or humanitarian logistics in the Horn of Africa.
yearly: Oman's jet/kerosene exports fell to 17.7 kbd in March 2026 (from a 2025 average of ~50 kbd), but this reflects seasonal variation and customer shifts more than crisis disruption, as Oman operates outside Hormuz. Djibouti emerged as the dominant buyer at 15.8 kbd/d -- likely supplying the US/coalition military buildup in the region.

The pre-crisis customer mix was split between European buyers (UK, Netherlands, France) and East African markets (Kenya, Mauritius, Madagascar). The March pivot to Djibouti and away from European routes suggests jet fuel is being redirected to military logistics rather than commercial aviation supply chains.

recent: Oman diesel exports tripled from ~49 kbd in 9–22 Mar (alternating between Pakistan and Singapore) to 179 kbd in 23–29 Mar as new African destinations appeared — Mozambique (94 kbd), Mauritius (47 kbd), and Singapore (37 kbd). The geographic reach into East Africa and Indian Ocean island states suggests Oman is filling the vacuum left by the collapse of UAE and Kuwait refined product exports.
The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr shows zero — likely a timing gap between cargoes rather than a structural drop, given the strong demand pull from these emerging markets.
quarterly: Oman's diesel exports continued post-crisis but at reduced and erratic volumes — averaging ~70 kbd in March versus ~160 kbd pre-crisis. African markets (Mozambique, Mauritius, South Africa) remain the primary buyers, with Singapore also appearing.

The decline is not from Hormuz disruption (Oman is outside the Strait) but from domestic refinery constraints — Oman's refining capacity is modest, and increased domestic demand likely diverted product from exports.
yearly: Oman's diesel exports moderated to 106 kbd in March 2026 from a 2025 average of ~160 kbd, but remained operational. Destinations shifted toward Southern Hemisphere markets (Mozambique 21 kbd/d, Singapore 20 kbd/d, South Africa 15 kbd/d) with no Gulf destinations -- indicating intra-Gulf diesel trade has ceased.

The continued flow, albeit reduced, reinforces Oman's strategic position as the only major Gulf refiner with unimpeded export access. Sohar refinery's inland production base (~950 kbd intact per Zawya) supports continued refined product output.

recent: Oman gasoline exports tripled from 43 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 123 kbd (23–29 Mar), directed entirely at Pakistan. Earlier weeks showed broader distribution to Tanzania, Sudan, and South Africa — all non-traditional destinations that have emerged as Oman fills the gap left by collapsed Saudi, UAE, and Kuwaiti refined product supply.
The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr shows zero, but the trend points to Oman's Sohar refinery becoming the de facto refined product hub for the Indian Ocean region during the crisis.
quarterly: Oman's gasoline exports (~80–120 kbd) continued through the crisis with Pakistan as the anchor buyer. Sudan and South Africa appeared as new destinations in March, suggesting these African markets are scrambling for alternative product sources as Kuwait and UAE gasoline disappeared.
The 0 kbd reading in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week may reflect timing rather than a shutdown — Oman's export infrastructure remains fully operational.
yearly: Oman's gasoline exports held at 88 kbd in March 2026, broadly consistent with the 2025 monthly average of ~110 kbd. However, the customer mix shifted dramatically: Saudi Arabia, which averaged 30-70 kbd/d in 2025 as Oman's largest gasoline buyer, disappeared entirely in March -- replaced by Pakistan (55 kbd/d) and East African markets.

The loss of Saudi Arabia as a buyer likely reflects Riyadh's decision to conserve Yanbu-routed refined products for domestic consumption and strategic exports, rather than purchasing from regional suppliers. Oman's gasoline surplus may face placement challenges as traditional Gulf interflow routes are severed.

recent: Oman naphtha exports were lumpy and single-cargo driven: 72 kbd to South Korea (W11), 29 kbd to India (W12), zero in W13, then a surge to 137 kbd in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr split between Japan (84 kbd) and Taiwan (53 kbd). Asian crackers facing FM declarations (FPCC at ~50% capacity, YNCC halted) are scrambling for any non-Hormuz naphtha barrel — Oman is the only Gulf supplier that can deliver.
quarterly: Oman naphtha exports were intermittent pre-crisis (~50–197 kbd) with South Korea as the dominant buyer. Post-crisis, volumes continued at ~30–137 kbd, but the buyer mix shifted notably: Japan (84 kbd) and Taiwan (53 kbd) appeared in the latest partial week, diverting from shut-in UAE and Kuwait naphtha supplies that previously fed Northeast Asian crackers.
yearly: Oman's naphtha exports remained stable at 59 kbd in March 2026 (vs 2025 average of ~75 kbd), continuing to serve Asian petchem feedstock demand. South Korea remained the primary buyer at 32 kbd/d, with India (17 kbd/d) and Taiwan (10 kbd/d) emerging as new buyers seeking alternatives to Gulf-sourced naphtha.

With naphtha prices surging past $1,000/mt and Formosa, YNCC, and Chandra Asri all declaring FM on naphtha-dependent operations, every available barrel outside Hormuz commands extreme premium. Early April shows a large 126 kbd/d flow to Japan -- a country that barely appeared in 2025 Oman naphtha data, signaling desperation for accessible feedstock.

recent: Qatar crude exports remain effectively at zero — all offshore fields (~440 kbd baseline) have been shut since early March under QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM declaration. A single ~117 kbd blip in 16–22 Mar (partially destined for UAE at 64 kbd) was likely a pre-loaded cargo or intra-Gulf inventory transfer, not new production.
With Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 both offline and QatarEnergy's CEO stating restart is impossible until the conflict ends entirely, Qatar crude has zero near-term recovery prospect.
quarterly: Qatar's crude exports collapsed from ~720 kbd average (Jan–Feb) to effectively zero after the first week of March. The last meaningful loadings were 292 kbd in 2–8 Mar (likely vessels already at berth when hostilities began); from 9 Mar onwards, only one small 117 kbd cargo to UAE appeared (16–22 Mar).

All Qatar offshore oil fields (~440 kbd) have been shut since early March, with Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 also offline. QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM declaration covers all hydrocarbon exports — crude restart is impossible until the conflict ends.
yearly: Qatar's crude exports collapsed from a 2025 average of ~830 kbd to just 179 kbd in March 2026 -- a 78% decline and effectively the end of Qatar's seaborne crude trade. All offshore oil fields (~440 kbd) shut since early March per Bloomberg, and Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 offline, consistent with QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM declaration.

The residual March volume went almost entirely to UAE (48 kbd/d) and South Korea (18 kbd/d) -- likely cargoes already loaded or in transit when the crisis began. April shows zero. The 2025 customer base was remarkably diverse (Singapore, Japan, South Korea, China, India, Thailand, Taiwan, UAE, Myanmar), serving as a key condensate hub. That entire trading network has been severed.

Qatar's crude loss is dwarfed by the LNG impact, but the elimination of ~440 kbd of light/condensate supply tightens the global light crude market at a time when Saudi medium/heavy and Russian Urals are the only available replacement barrels.

recent: A trickle of Qatar LNG has resumed — rising from zero (W11) to 6.5 ktons/d to Kuwait in W12, then 18.4 ktons/d split between Kuwait and Pakistan in 23–29 Mar, and 11.4 ktons/d to Kuwait in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. This is less than 9% of Qatar's pre-crisis ~211 ktons/d capacity (77 Mtpa).

These intra-Gulf movements to Kuwait (for Al-Zour power generation) and Pakistan don't transit the Strait of Hormuz, explaining how they're possible despite the FM declaration. Kuwait's desperate need for gas-fired power generation is driving this emergency supply arrangement.

The MOL LPG carrier Green Sanvi successfully transiting Hormuz to India on Apr 4 (per S&P Platts) may signal the beginning of limited, escorted Qatari shipments through the Strait — a development to watch closely in coming weeks.
quarterly: Qatar's LNG exports — which averaged ~232 ktons/day (~77 Mtpa) through January and February, representing ~20% of global LNG supply — collapsed to near-zero by mid-March. The 2–8 Mar week showed 53 ktons/day (final cargoes clearing), then effectively zero from 9 Mar onwards.

The only post-crisis LNG movements are tiny volumes to Kuwait (7–11 ktons/day in late March), likely emergency deliveries to keep Al-Zour FSRU partially running for Kuwaiti power generation — a stark reversal for the world's largest LNG exporter.

QatarEnergy extended FM through mid-June to Edison (Italy), Belgium, South Korea, and China on 24–27 Mar. With 12.8 Mtpa of capacity physically damaged (3–5 year rebuild per OilPrice), even a ceasefire would not restore full volumes quickly. Golden Pass LNG (Texas, QatarEnergy 70%) ramping first cargo in April provides marginal offset.
yearly: Qatar's LNG exports -- the single most consequential commodity disruption in this crisis -- collapsed from a 2025 average of ~220 ktons/d (equivalent to ~77 Mtpa, ~20% of global LNG supply) to just 26 ktons/d in March 2026, an 88% reduction. The only remaining deliveries went to Kuwait (3.7 ktons/d -- likely from stored cargoes) and Pakistan (2 ktons/d). Early April shows just 17 ktons/d to Kuwait.

The FM, extended on March 24 to Italy, Belgium, South Korea, and China, and on March 27 to Edison (10 cargoes through mid-June), has removed the equivalent of the entire US LNG export capacity from global markets. The pre-crisis customer base spanned 15+ countries across Asia and Europe; now it's effectively zero.

QatarEnergy's 12.8 Mtpa of damaged infrastructure requires 3-5 years to restore even after the conflict ends. The first LNG carrier (MOL's Sohar LNG) attempted Hormuz transit on April 3, and Golden Pass LNG (QatarEnergy 70%, Texas) is ramping its first cargo -- but these are symbolic volumes against a 77 Mtpa shortfall. Asian spot LNG at $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis) reflects a market in structural deficit that will persist for years.

recent: Saudi crude exports ramped from ~3.9 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to a peak of ~6.2 mb/d (23–29 Mar) — recovering to ~87% of the pre-war 7.1 mb/d — entirely via the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu at its 7 mb/d maximum capacity (~5 mb/d exported, ~2 mb/d to Red Sea refineries). The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr shows ~5.0 mb/d, suggesting the pipeline throughput is stabilizing near its structural ceiling.

Egypt has become the top destination at 1.5–1.9 mb/d across all weeks — serving as the SUMED pipeline transit hub for onward shipment to Mediterranean and Asian buyers. China (1.4–1.7 mb/d) and India (0.9–1.3 mb/d) are consistently the largest end-market buyers, while Myanmar, Thailand, and Malaysia have appeared as new first-time Saudi crude destinations.

The pipeline bypass is working but capped — OPEC+ confirmed a symbolic 206 kbd increase on Apr 5, but Saudi Arabia physically cannot push more than ~7 mb/d through Yanbu. Any further export recovery requires Hormuz reopening or completion of the long-discussed Yanbu expansion.
quarterly: Saudi crude exports fell ~30% from a Jan–Feb average of ~7.1 mb/d to ~5.1 mb/d in March, with the Yanbu pipeline bypass running at its 7 mb/d maximum capacity — but terminal constraints at Yanbu (estimated 3–4 mb/d effective loading per Vortexa) cap actual export throughput.

The customer mix underwent a radical reshuffling: Egypt surged from ~700 kbd pre-crisis to 1.5–2.3 mb/d (Red Sea proximity to Yanbu terminal makes it the natural destination), while Japan — previously taking 1.0–1.8 mb/d — virtually disappeared from the buyer list by late March.

China and India each absorbed 1.0–1.7 mb/d in March, roughly in line with pre-crisis volumes, suggesting these buyers are prioritising Saudi barrels via longer Red Sea/Suez routings. South Korea maintained ~300–845 kbd, while Myanmar and Pakistan emerged as new buyers.

The 9–15 Mar dip to 3.9 mb/d likely reflected the chaotic transition to full Yanbu operations; subsequent weeks stabilized at 5.0–6.2 mb/d. With OPEC+ confirming a 206 kbd increase on 5 Apr, Saudi production policy signals confidence — but the pipeline ceiling means export recovery is structurally capped until Hormuz reopens.
yearly: Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline bypass is the defining infrastructure story of this crisis: crude exports fell from a pre-war peak of 7.28 mb/d (Feb 2026) to 5.42 mb/d in March -- a 26% decline, but far less severe than the 78-81% collapses seen in Qatar, Kuwait, and Iraq. The pipeline, now at its max 7 mb/d throughput per Fortune, reroutes crude from Eastern Province fields to Yanbu on the Red Sea.

The customer reshuffle is dramatic: Egypt surged to the #1 destination at 1.74 mb/d (from ~936 kbd/d in Feb) via the SUMED pipeline, replacing Japan which collapsed from 1.32 mb/d to just 103 kbd/d. China held at 1.07 mb/d, India at 869 kbd/d, and South Korea fell from 908 to 363 kbd/d. The US, which bought 252 kbd/d in February, disappeared entirely in March.

The geographic routing shift is structural: Yanbu can serve Red Sea, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean destinations but struggles to compete for East Asian buyers (Japan, South Korea, Taiwan) who previously received cargoes via Hormuz. Myanmar appeared at 194 kbd/d in March -- a rare new transit routing via Indian Ocean. Saudi crude has effectively pivoted from a Pacific-facing to an Atlantic/Indian Ocean-facing export orientation.

Early April (4 days, 5.16 mb/d) shows China surging to 2.0 mb/d, South Korea at 766 kbd/d, and Egypt at 1.5 mb/d -- suggesting Yanbu loading operations are stabilizing. But at max pipeline capacity, Saudi Arabia has permanently lost ~1.5-2 mb/d of pre-war export capacity until Hormuz reopens.

recent: Saudi jet fuel exports were erratic — 46 kbd (W11), spiking to 95 kbd to the UK in 16–22 Mar, then zero in 23–29 Mar, recovering to 57 kbd in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. The UK cargo likely reflected an emergency supply arrangement for European aviation fuel as Gulf jet supplies collapsed.

The WoW volatility reflects limited and intermittent Red Sea shipping windows rather than production constraints — Saudi refineries at Yanbu have jet fuel output capacity but face logistics bottlenecks routing everything through a single port complex.
quarterly: Saudi jet fuel/kerosene exports dropped sharply from a pre-crisis average of ~137 kbd to near-zero in March. The UK — previously the dominant buyer at ~50–120 kbd — received only sporadic cargoes (95 kbd in one late-Feb week, then largely nothing).
The collapse reflects both Yanbu pipeline prioritization of crude over products and the general disruption of long-haul refined product logistics from the Red Sea.
yearly: Saudi jet/kerosene exports, which averaged ~110 kbd through 2025 and were heavily oriented toward the UAE (30-44 kbd/d) and European military logistics (UK, France, Cyprus), face severe disruption. The UAE as a destination has been eliminated by the Hormuz closure.

The Yanbu pivot likely redirects residual jet fuel output toward Red Sea and Mediterranean military customers (Djibouti for the US/coalition, Jordan for regional logistics). The loss of UAE-bound jet fuel compounds the Fujairah bunkering hub crisis, where MEE and Pearl Marine both declared FM on marine fuel deliveries.

recent: Saudi diesel exports sustained at 380–667 kbd across all four weeks, with an extraordinarily diverse destination base spanning 15+ countries: Europe (Italy, Spain, Netherlands, Belgium, Slovenia), Africa (Kenya, Djibouti, Libya), and Asia (Pakistan, Malaysia, Egypt). This diversity signals Saudi Arabia's Yanbu-based product export infrastructure is functioning as the primary crisis-era diesel supply hub for three continents.

Kenya emerged as the single largest diesel destination in 23–29 Mar at 153 kbd, surpassing Egypt (113 kbd) — reflecting East African buyers who previously sourced from UAE or Kuwait now pivoting entirely to Saudi Red Sea cargoes. The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr holds steady at ~621 kbd with Pakistan as the only named destination.
quarterly: Saudi diesel exports proved more resilient than other refined products — declining from a Jan–Feb average of ~730 kbd to ~570 kbd in March, a ~22% drop. The Yanbu refinery complex continues processing, enabling some product export alongside crude.

The customer base shifted dramatically westward and southward: Europe (Italy, Spain, France, Belgium) largely disappeared by late March, replaced by East African and Middle Eastern buyers — Kenya surged to 153 kbd (23–29 Mar), Egypt maintained ~110–140 kbd, and Pakistan emerged as a key destination at 116 kbd in the latest partial week.
yearly: Saudi diesel exports averaged ~300 kbd through 2025, serving a global customer base spanning Asia (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Singapore) and East Africa (Djibouti, Somalia). March 2026 flows likely shifted entirely to Yanbu-accessible destinations -- Red Sea, Mediterranean, and East African markets.

The structural shift mirrors crude: customers reachable via Red Sea routing (Egypt, Djibouti, East Africa, Europe) will retain access, while Asia-Pacific buyers (Singapore, India) face longer voyage times and higher freight costs to receive Saudi diesel via the Cape route. This compounds the global middle distillate tightness from lost Kuwait and UAE refinery output.

recent: Saudi gasoline exports averaged ~256 kbd across the four weeks, peaking at 361 kbd in 16–22 Mar. Singapore has been the most consistent hub buyer (43–138 kbd weekly), serving as a redistribution point for Southeast Asian markets. African destinations (Kenya, Sudan, South Africa) feature prominently across all weeks.

Notably, Bahrain appeared as a gasoline buyer for the first time in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr at 55 kbd — a direct consequence of the Sitra refinery's destruction by Iranian missile strikes, forcing Bahrain to import refined products from its Saudi neighbor via the King Fahd Causeway supply chain.
quarterly: Saudi gasoline exports halved from a pre-crisis average of ~325 kbd to ~165 kbd through March. Gulf neighbour shipments (Oman, UAE, Qatar — which took 100–200 kbd pre-crisis) largely ceased as those countries' own demand collapsed alongside refinery shutdowns.

Singapore and Malaysia became the dominant remaining buyers at 40–140 kbd. One notable cargo: 55 kbd to Bahrain in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week — likely emergency fuel for a country whose entire Sitra refinery (405 kbd) was destroyed by Iranian missile strikes.
yearly: Saudi gasoline exports averaged ~130 kbd through mid-2025, primarily serving Pakistan, East Africa (Djibouti, Kenya, Tanzania), and some European destinations. The Yanbu rerouting constrains volumes but maintains access to Red Sea-adjacent markets.

With Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi refinery destroyed and Bahrain's Sitra offline (405 kbd), Saudi Arabia has lost its two closest sources of refined product imports for domestic balancing. The kingdom is likely consuming a larger share of its own refinery output domestically, constraining export availability even as Yanbu port capacity becomes the binding constraint.

recent: Saudi naphtha exports fluctuated between 73–171 kbd, with Japan and Singapore as the primary buyers. The spike to 171 kbd in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr (Japan taking 84 kbd) reflects desperate Asian petchem feedstock procurement — naphtha has surged past $1,000/mt per Formosa Plastics' FM declaration, and any Red Sea-accessible barrel commands extreme premiums.

Indonesia (52 kbd in W11) and Singapore (21–73 kbd across weeks) round out the buyer base, consistent with Southeast Asian crackers scrambling to replace the ~2+ mb/d of Gulf naphtha supply now offline.
quarterly: Saudi naphtha exports remained broadly stable at ~70–170 kbd, with the Yanbu refinery complex generating sufficient volumes. The customer mix shifted: Thailand (previously dominant at ~70 kbd) faded, while Japan appeared as a significant new buyer at 84 kbd in the latest partial week — Japanese crackers desperately sourcing non-Hormuz naphtha after UAE and Kuwait supplies vanished.

Indonesia and Singapore continued as regular buyers. South Korea maintained intermittent purchases, reflecting YNCC's FM declaration on ethylene production due to Middle East naphtha supply suspension.
yearly: Saudi naphtha exports through 2025 served as a critical feedstock for Asian petrochemical crackers, with Japan, South Korea, India, and UAE as primary buyers. The Hormuz closure redirects whatever naphtha surplus remains through Yanbu, but this product is likely deprioritized in the pipeline capacity queue behind crude oil and diesel.

With global naphtha above $1,000/mt triggering FM declarations across Asian crackers (Formosa, YNCC, Chandra Asri, Wanhua), every barrel of Saudi naphtha that can reach Asia commands extreme premiums -- but pipeline capacity constraints mean volumes will be far below pre-war levels.

recent: UAE crude exports showed extreme WoW volatility: 2.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar) → 1.1 (16–22 Mar) → 3.1 (23–29 Mar) → 1.2 in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. The W13 surge to 3.1 mb/d was remarkable, featuring first-time buyers Philippines (396 kbd) and Vietnam (257 kbd) — consistent with ADCOP pipeline throughput to Fujairah (~1.6 mb/d) supplemented by occasional Hormuz-transiting cargoes.

India and South Korea have emerged as the most consistent UAE crude buyers (300–586 kbd and 333–529 kbd respectively), while the reappearance of Oman as a destination (257 kbd in W12, 119 kbd in partial W14) suggests intra-regional crude transfers — possibly for blending at Oman's export terminals outside Hormuz.

The Habshan gas facility's second wartime shutdown on Apr 3 and Ruwais Refinery-2 remaining offline suggest UAE crude-for-export volumes will remain constrained. S&P Platts' new Inside/Outside Strait crude pricing bifurcation (launched Apr 1) formally recognizes the structural split between ADCOP-accessible and Hormuz-trapped barrels.
quarterly: UAE crude exports declined ~50% from a Jan–Feb average of ~3.3 mb/d to ~1.7 mb/d in March, with extreme week-to-week volatility: a low of 1.1 mb/d (16–22 Mar) followed by a 3.1 mb/d spike (23–29 Mar), then back to 1.2 mb/d in the partial latest week. This lumpiness reflects VLCC scheduling through the ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah (outside Hormuz).

ADCOP's ~1.6 mb/d capacity (per S&P Platts Inside/Outside Strait bifurcation launched 1 Apr) provides a partial bypass but is far smaller than Saudi Arabia's 7 mb/d Yanbu pipeline, limiting UAE's crisis-era export ceiling.

The buyer base diversified under stress: India surged to become the #1 customer (385–586 kbd in late March), South Korea held at 330–514 kbd, and new buyers appeared — Philippines (396 kbd, 23–29 Mar), Vietnam (257 kbd), and Pakistan. Japan — previously taking 600–1,350 kbd — dropped to 170–260 kbd as longer transit times from Fujairah (vs direct Gulf loading) make it uncompetitive against closer suppliers.
yearly: UAE crude exports fell from a pre-crisis average of 3.29 mb/d (2025) to 2.12 mb/d in March 2026 -- a 37% decline cushioned by the ADCOP Habshan-Fujairah pipeline, which bypasses Hormuz to load tankers on the Gulf of Oman coast. Fujairah loadings averaged ~1.9 mb/d in late March per World Oil, up 57% from the 2025 average, with the pipeline operating at 71% of its 1.5 mb/d nameplate (surge capacity to 1.8 mb/d).

The customer reshuffle is striking: Japan collapsed from ~869 kbd/d (2025 avg) to 148 kbd/d in March, China fell from ~670 to 177 kbd/d, while South Korea surged to #1 at 336 kbd/d, Thailand held at 290 kbd/d, and India rose to 246 kbd/d. New destinations appeared: Philippines (89 kbd/d), Pakistan (79 kbd/d), Vietnam (58 kbd/d) -- reflecting emergency sourcing by smaller Asian importers.

Early April (1.63 mb/d in 4 days) shows further contraction, with India (497 kbd/d) and South Korea (499 kbd/d) dominant. The second Habshan gas shutdown on April 3 (Bloomberg) -- with 1 Egyptian killed, 4 injured -- underscores that even Fujairah-routed exports face upstream production risks. With Shah Gas Field still shut (~1 Bcf/d) and Ruwais Refinery-2 offline, UAE is exporting crude that it can no longer refine domestically.
Oman appeared as a destination in March (58 kbd/d) for the first time at meaningful volumes -- likely reflecting Omani storage and transhipment as an emerging bypass hub for Hormuz-locked barrels.

recent: UAE diesel exports effectively ceased after a single 73 kbd cargo to Pakistan in 9–15 Mar — zero across all subsequent weeks including the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr. With Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and the Habshan gas facility struck again on Apr 3, the UAE has lost its refined product export capability entirely.
This is a stark reversal for what was the Gulf's second-largest product export hub; regional buyers have pivoted to Saudi Yanbu and Oman as replacement sources.
quarterly: UAE diesel/gasoil exports collapsed completely — from a pre-crisis average of ~180 kbd (serving African markets: Tanzania, Kenya, Ghana, Mozambique) to zero from 16 Mar onwards. The last trickle was 73 kbd to Pakistan (9–15 Mar).

With Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Habshan gas plant shut again on 3 Apr (Bloomberg — 1 Egyptian killed, 4 injured), UAE's downstream product export capacity is effectively eliminated for the foreseeable future.
yearly: UAE diesel exports averaged ~210 kbd through 2025, serving a diverse customer base spanning South Asia (India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka), Southeast Asia (Singapore, Philippines), and East Africa. The Ruwais Refinery-2 shutdown and Hormuz closure have fundamentally disrupted both production and routing.

March 2026 volumes likely dropped sharply, with Fujairah port handling whatever reduced Ruwais output remains. The loss of UAE diesel compounds a regional refined products crisis: with Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi and Bahrain's Sitra also offline, approximately 800+ kbd of Gulf refining capacity has been destroyed or shut, creating a structural middle distillate shortage for the Indian Ocean region.

recent: UAE naphtha exports were minimal and sporadic: zero for the first two weeks, then a single 46 kbd cargo to Taiwan in 23–29 Mar, followed by 95 kbd in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr (Kenya at 34 kbd visible). These isolated cargoes likely reflect small-lot inventory clearance from Fujairah rather than sustained refinery production, given the Ruwais and Habshan shutdowns.
Taiwan's appearance as a buyer (W13) is consistent with FPCC's desperate naphtha procurement — their crackers are at ~50% capacity with FM declared on multiple downstream products.
quarterly: UAE naphtha exports collapsed from a pre-crisis average of ~490 kbd — feeding Asian petrochemical crackers in Japan, South Korea, China, Taiwan, and Singapore — to near-zero by mid-March. The 91 kbd in 2–8 Mar was the last meaningful volume; subsequent weeks showed 0–95 kbd.

This shutdown has directly triggered FM declarations across Asia's petrochemical chain: Formosa Plastics (Taiwan), YNCC (South Korea), and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) all cited Middle East naphtha loss. Naphtha prices surged past $1,000/mt, with Asian crackers cutting to ~50% utilisation rates.
yearly: UAE naphtha exports through 2025 were modest (~60-100 kbd), primarily serving Asian petrochemical crackers in Japan, South Korea, and India. The Hormuz closure and Ruwais shutdown have likely reduced these to near-zero for seaborne volumes.

Any residual UAE naphtha production routes via Fujairah, but with crude oil taking priority in pipeline capacity, naphtha export allocation is minimal. This adds to the global naphtha feedstock crisis driving cracker shutdowns across Asia.

recent: EU-27 crude re-exports are negligible and erratic at best -- averaging just 0.14-0.20 mb/d in 9-15 Mar and 23-29 Mar, with a near-zero 46 kbd trickle in 16-22 Mar. Finland and the UK account for essentially all volume, reflecting sporadic intra-European cargo movements rather than any structural export flow.

Zero Gulf share across all four weeks confirms that EU refineries are retaining every barrel of crude they can source, consistent with the 50%+ crude price surge and the EU's acute dependence on Middle East refined products documented by the IEA.
quarterly: EU-27 crude re-exports are negligible and sporadic — averaging just 60–100 kbd across Q1 2026, with volumes driven by intra-European redistribution (Finland, Sweden, Netherlands, UK) rather than meaningful international trade.
The post-crisis spike to 198 kbd in 9–15 Mar reflects one-off Finnish and UK cargo movements, not a structural trend; the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr recorded zero loadings.
yearly: EU-27 crude re-exports doubled to ~111 kbd in March 2026 — the 12-month high — driven by a surge in Finland-bound cargoes (58 kbd, tripling its 2025 average), likely reflecting refined product blending demand as European refiners scramble for non-Gulf feedstock.

Full-year volumes averaged just 48 kbd — negligible in global terms — with no Gulf exposure at any point, confirming EU crude re-exports are entirely North Sea/Baltic sourced and insulated from the Hormuz disruption.
April partial (4 days) shows zero flow, consistent with the episodic, cargo-by-cargo nature of these small re-export trades.

recent: EU jet fuel exports collapsed 75% WoW to just 37 kbd in 23-29 Mar from 141 kbd in 16-22 Mar, before partially recovering to ~104 kbd in the 6-day partial week of 30 Mar-4 Apr. The drop reflects European refiners diverting jet output to domestic supply as Gulf middle distillate exports (3.3 mb/d pre-crisis) remain fully shut.

Destination mix narrowed sharply -- from 8 countries in 16-22 Mar (UK, Spain, Norway, Cyprus, Malta, Albania, South Africa, Latvia) to just 3-4 in subsequent weeks, with the UK consistently the anchor buyer, signalling tightening European aviation fuel balances.
quarterly: EU-27 jet fuel exports declined ~32% from a pre-crisis average of 148 kbd to ~101 kbd through March, consistent with European refiners prioritising domestic supply as Gulf-sourced middle distillate imports contracted — the UK, historically the top destination at 55–114 kbd, halved its intake.

Destination diversification accelerated: France, Spain, and Norway absorbed increased shares through March, while new buyers (Latvia, Albania) appeared — suggesting European refiners are re-routing surplus jet between neighbours rather than exporting long-haul.
The trajectory is volatile (77 kbd in early March → 146 kbd mid-March → 37 kbd in 23–29 Mar), reflecting lumpy cargo scheduling rather than a clear trend.
yearly: EU-27 jet fuel exports averaged ~120 kbd across the 12-month window, peaking at 187 kbd in February 2026 — the seasonal pre-summer aviation stockbuild — before easing to 105 kbd in March as the Hormuz crisis redirected refinery focus toward diesel and gasoline.

The UK remains the structural anchor buyer at 35–76 kbd monthly, absorbing 35–45% of all EU jet exports throughout the period — a reflection of the UK's continued reliance on continental refinery surplus post-Brexit.

France surged to 19 kbd in March 2026 (from near-zero in Feb), alongside Belgium's first appearance in 12 months, signalling intra-European redistribution of jet supplies as Gulf-sourced kerosene deliveries to Mediterranean hubs collapsed.

recent: EU diesel exports dropped 37% from ~1.18 mb/d in 16-22 Mar to ~0.71 mb/d in 23-29 Mar and held at that level in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr (~0.71 mb/d), reflecting the squeeze as Gulf refining capacity (3+ mb/d offline from Bapco Sitra, Kuwait Al-Ahmadi strikes, Iraq Basra shutdowns) removes a major global diesel supply source.

Buyer geography is shifting south -- South Africa surged to 282 kbd (top buyer) in the partial 30 Mar-4 Apr week, and Namibia appeared at 172 kbd, consistent with Cape-routed vessels bypassing Hormuz and African nations scrambling for product as Gulf flows dry up. Poland and UK remain consistent European anchors at 50-195 kbd.
quarterly: EU-27 diesel/gasoil exports held broadly stable at ~900–940 kbd across Q1 2026, indicating European refineries are running near-normal throughput despite elevated crude input costs — the crisis hasn't yet forced significant European refinery cutbacks.

Post-crisis volumes actually spiked to 1,183 kbd in 16–22 Mar as Polish and UK demand surged, before easing to ~712 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr; Poland's emergence as a top-2 destination (up from #5 pre-crisis) signals Central European stockbuilding ahead of potential Russian product supply disruptions.
South Africa and Croatia appeared as new post-crisis destinations — consistent with Southern Hemisphere buyers scrambling for non-Gulf diesel as Middle East refinery output collapsed.
yearly: EU-27 diesel exports held remarkably steady at 880–1,048 kbd through the 12-month period, averaging ~950 kbd — demonstrating structural resilience as Europe's refined product surplus flows westward to the UK (120–140 kbd), North/West Africa, and the Balkans regardless of Gulf conditions.

March 2026 saw Poland surge to second position at 136 kbd (up from 32 kbd in Jan), while Romania quadrupled to 48 kbd — pointing to increased Eastern European refinery throughput as Russian diesel ban fears drive pre-emptive European output maximisation.

South Africa appeared in the top-5 for the first time in the April partial (86 kbd daily), alongside Norway's jump to 34 kbd — new demand corridors opening as Middle Eastern product supplies to Africa and the Atlantic Basin evaporate.

recent: EU gasoline exports actually rose through March -- from 1.14 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to 1.41 mb/d (23-29 Mar) -- as European refiners pivot gasoline surplus toward global deficit markets. The 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr ran at ~0.89 mb/d, on pace to match prior weeks.

Africa and the Middle East have emerged as major new customers: Egypt surged to 151 kbd, Kenya held at ~111 kbd, and Senegal/Ghana/Libya featured prominently -- filling the vacuum left by collapsed Gulf product exports. Saudi Arabia took 37 kbd of EU gasoline in 9-15 Mar, a striking reversal for a traditional exporter, consistent with KPC and Bapco refinery shutdowns constraining regional supply.

EU refiners are likely running at elevated utilisation to capture record crack spreads, but the southward tilt in gasoline flows (South Africa 282 kbd in partial week, Namibia, Ivory Coast appearing) signals that Cape-routed tanker economics now favour Atlantic Basin-to-Africa routes over traditional intra-European trades.
quarterly: EU-27 gasoline exports held steady at ~1.15 mb/d through Q1, but the destination map has been radically redrawn: Saudi Arabia, which was absorbing 45–224 kbd pre-crisis (as much as 16% of total), virtually disappeared after the Hormuz closure — only a residual 37 kbd cargo reached the Kingdom in 9–15 Mar.

Africa has surged as the primary outlet: South Africa (282 kbd), Kenya (111 kbd), Egypt (151 kbd), and Namibia (172 kbd) collectively absorbed volumes that previously flowed to the Gulf, as African nations diversify away from now-disrupted Middle East product supply.

Libya remains a consistent buyer throughout Q1 (111–198 kbd), benefiting from Mediterranean proximity and suggesting Libyan refineries continue to supplement domestic production with European gasoline — a structural flow predating the crisis.
yearly: EU-27 gasoline exports averaged ~1,190 kbd over 12 months, with a dramatic geographic reshuffling during the crisis: Saudi Arabia spiked to 154 kbd (12% share) in January 2026 as pre-conflict stockpiling peaked, then collapsed to just 8 kbd (0.7% share) by March as Hormuz shut entirely.

The US, historically the top buyer at 166–294 kbd through mid-2025, faded to 58 kbd in March 2026 as US Gulf Coast crack spreads surged past $54/bbl — American refiners are running at full capacity and no longer need European gasoline imports.

Africa emerged as the crisis-era growth market: South Africa (56 kbd), Kenya (50 kbd), and Egypt (58 kbd) entered the March top-10 as Gulf product supply to the continent dried up, with the April partial showing South Africa and Namibia dominating at 337 and 258 kbd respectively — a wholesale trade reorientation.

recent: EU naphtha exports held stable at ~395-413 kbd through 9-29 Mar but cratered to just 117 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr, with only France, Spain, and Belgium receiving cargoes. The Asian petrochemical feedstock scramble is intensifying -- Japan surged to 103 kbd (23-29 Mar) and China appeared at 49 kbd, both unusual EU naphtha buyers, consistent with Formosa FPCC and YNCC FM declarations citing total Middle East naphtha supply suspension.

South Korea's naphtha buying shifted from 73 kbd of EU naphtha (9-15 Mar) to US naphtha (251 kbd in 16-22 Mar), illustrating how Asian crackers are aggressively arbitraging Atlantic Basin feedstock to replace Gulf supply lost since the Hormuz closure.
quarterly: EU-27 naphtha exports contracted 16% from a Q1 pre-crisis average of ~391 kbd to ~326 kbd through March, with the steepest drop in the partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr (117 kbd) — reflecting tightening European feedstock balances as Gulf naphtha supply evaporated.

Asian buyers emerged as crisis-period destinations: Japan (103 kbd in 23–29 Mar), South Korea, and China all appeared in March data, replacing the traditional Netherlands-Belgium intra-EU redistribution pattern — European refiners are exporting naphtha eastward at premium pricing as Asian crackers compete for any non-Gulf barrel.

The structural pressure is building: with Formosa Plastics, YNCC, and Chandra Asri all declaring FM on naphtha-dependent operations, Asian naphtha demand is pulling European molecules east, which could tighten EU petrochemical feedstock in Q2.
yearly: EU-27 naphtha exports averaged ~370 kbd through the year, with Asian crackers as key swing buyers: South Korea took 34 kbd in June 2025 and surged to 62 kbd in August — peak pre-crisis Asian demand — before disappearing entirely from March 2026 as YNCC and other Korean crackers declared FM and halted naphtha purchases.

Belgium and Netherlands remain the structural core of intra-European naphtha trade (combined 100–150 kbd), feeding ARA-region steam crackers. March 2026 volumes at 352 kbd are 25% below the August 2025 peak of 471 kbd, reflecting reduced downstream petrochemical demand as Formosa Plastics, LyondellBasell, and other crackers cut rates globally.

The naphtha price surge past $1,000/mt — driven by Gulf feedstock loss — is paradoxically suppressing European export volumes as crackers across Asia declare force majeure rather than pay crisis-inflated prices.

recent: Russian crude exports declined 20% over the month -- from 6.7 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to 5.4 mb/d (23-29 Mar), running at ~4.9 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr. The US sanctions waiver (Mar 12-Apr 11) is supporting flows but China's offtake dropped sharply from 2.3 mb/d to 1.1 mb/d, while India fell from 1.7 mb/d to 0.8 mb/d, suggesting logistics and port congestion constraints rather than demand weakness.

European buyers remain stubbornly active despite sanctions: Italy (~660-714 kbd), France (~177-455 kbd), Netherlands (~153-252 kbd), and new appearances by Spain (176 kbd) and Poland (129 kbd) in the partial week. CREA data shows 56% of Russian crude now moves on shadow fleet vessels, limiting Western leverage.

Customer diversification continued with the Philippines (212 kbd, 16-22 Mar), Hong Kong (~73-117 kbd), and Georgia (~90 kbd) all appearing as new or expanded buyers -- reflecting the scramble for non-Gulf barrels as Hormuz removes 12-15 mb/d from global supply.
quarterly: Russian crude exports surged 9% to an average of ~5.67 mb/d across the crisis period (2–29 Mar), peaking at a remarkable 6.72 mb/d in 9–15 Mar — the highest weekly rate in Q1 — as the US sanctions waiver (12 Mar–11 Apr) and the Gulf supply vacuum created a structural pull for Russian barrels.

China remains the anchor buyer at ~1.6–2.3 mb/d consistently through Q1, while India doubled down: Indian imports spiked to 1.68 mb/d in 9–15 Mar (vs. 0.65 mb/d in early January), consistent with India's reported purchase of 30M+ barrels in a single week as refiners scrambled for non-Gulf crude.

New buyers signal Russia's expanding customer base: Israel appeared at 307 kbd in 2–8 Mar (unprecedented), France absorbed 1.28 mb/d cumulatively across the crisis period, and Italy maintained ~660+ kbd — European buyers quietly replacing Gulf crude with Russian barrels despite sanctions optics.

The trajectory has softened from the March peak: 5.37 mb/d in 23–29 Mar and 4.93 mb/d in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr, suggesting the initial panic-buying surge is normalising, though absolute levels remain well above the Q1 pre-crisis average of 5.2 mb/d.
yearly: Russian seaborne crude exports averaged 5.6 mb/d over 12 months, with a structural shift toward Asia accelerating during the crisis: China absorbed 1.8–2.3 mb/d consistently (doubling YoY per SCMP reporting), while India surged from ~1.5 mb/d pre-crisis to become the swing buyer as Gulf supplies collapsed.

The January–February 2026 dip to ~5.0 mb/d reflected tighter Western sanctions enforcement — only 33% of exports carried on G7+ tankers by Feb, with 56% on shadow fleet vessels — but March rebounded sharply to 5.85 mb/d as India purchased 30M+ barrels in a single week under the US sanctions waiver (Mar 12–Apr 11).

Italy's persistent presence as the #3 destination (200–450 kbd monthly) underscores the incomplete nature of EU sanctions: Mediterranean refiners continue to access Russian Urals despite the price cap, exploiting ESPO discounts of ~$16/bbl below benchmarks.
The Hormuz crisis has structurally entrenched Russia as Asia's indispensable marginal supplier — reversing four years of Western policy aimed at isolating Russian crude from global markets.

recent: Russian LNG exports fluctuated between 68-111 ktons/d across the four weeks, with the EU ban on Russian LNG imports (effective April 25) looming. France and Belgium remained the largest European buyers at 10-31 ktons/d each, consistent with CREA data showing Yamal LNG sent 100% of Feb exports to EU.

Asian buyers are increasing: Japan took 18-19 ktons/d in two of four weeks, South Korea appeared at ~9 ktons/d, and China ranged 9-28 ktons/d. With Qatar's 77 Mtpa fully offline and Asian spot LNG at $20+/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis), Russian Arctic cargoes command significant premium -- the approaching EU ban may simply redirect volumes eastward.

At ~80-110 ktons/d (~29-40 Mt/yr annualised), Russian LNG output remains well below nameplate capacity, suggesting Arctic LNG-2 continues to underperform and Yamal alone cannot fully capitalise on the Qatar gap.
quarterly: Russian LNG exports dipped ~9% to an average of 90 ktons/day across the crisis period, slightly below the pre-crisis Q1 average of 99 ktons/day — surprising given Qatar's 77 Mtpa is fully offline and Asian spot prices have surged ~39%.

The underperformance reflects infrastructure constraints rather than lack of demand: Arctic LNG-2 remains below nameplate capacity, and Yamal LNG is physically maxed out — Russia simply cannot ramp LNG exports to fill the Qatari gap.

France remains the single largest buyer (~25–32 ktons/day consistently), with Spain and Belgium rounding out the top 3 — but the impending EU ban on Russian LNG purchases (effective 25 Apr) threatens to strand ~60% of Russian LNG export volumes, potentially forcing a chaotic reorientation toward Asian spot markets.

The downtrend is accelerating: 80 ktons/day in 23–29 Mar and 68 ktons/day in the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr, the lowest weekly rate in Q1 — early evidence of European buyers pre-emptively cutting Russian LNG ahead of the April 25 ban.
yearly: Russian LNG exports grew 20% from the mid-2025 trough (62 ktons/d in August) to 103 ktons/d in January 2026, directly benefiting from Qatar's 77 Mtpa going offline — but volumes have since eased to 93 ktons/d in March as Arctic LNG-2 continues to underperform nameplate capacity.

France remains the paradoxical top buyer, taking 15–25 ktons/d throughout the period despite Paris's vocal support for the EU Russian LNG ban that takes effect April 25, 2026 — Yamal LNG sent 100% of February exports to the EU per CREA tracking.

The April 25 EU import ban will forcibly redirect ~50–60 ktons/d of Russian LNG toward Asian spot markets where JKM has surged to $20+/MMBtu — a windfall for Novatek but a further tightening of European gas supply.

recent: Russian diesel exports fell 64% from ~1.0 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to just 360 kbd (23-29 Mar), before rebounding to ~690 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr. Turkey dominates at 140-526 kbd, essentially functioning as Russia's primary diesel outlet, with Brazil the second-largest buyer (~108-203 kbd).

The W13 (23-29 Mar) trough coincided with the end of a Turkish refinery turnaround cycle and seasonal domestic demand, while the W14 rebound to Turkey (526 kbd) suggests a return to full Turkish offtake. Syria's 64 kbd in 9-15 Mar reflects Russia's continued wartime diesel supply to Damascus, a flow invisible to most Western sanctions enforcement.
quarterly: Russian diesel/gasoil exports averaged ~723 kbd during the crisis period, down 10% from the pre-crisis average of 806 kbd, with Turkey remaining the dominant buyer at 140–526 kbd weekly — Ankara continues to serve as a key transit hub for Russian refined products.

The sharp dip to 360 kbd in 23–29 Mar (the lowest point in Q1) coincided with Russia's domestic fuel supply management ahead of the April 1 gasoline export ban, before recovering to 690 kbd in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week — suggesting the gasoil/diesel flow was temporarily disrupted by refinery throughput reallocation.

New crisis-era destinations include Sudan (82–114 kbd in early March) and Malaysia (111 kbd in 9–15 Mar), signalling Russian products reaching markets that previously sourced from Gulf refineries like Bapco Sitra (now offline after drone strikes).
yearly: Russian diesel exports averaged ~780 kbd over 12 months, with Turkey as the structural anchor at 150–240 kbd monthly — effectively serving as Russia's primary refined product outlet after the EU embargo, with Turkish refiners blending and re-exporting as 'Turkish origin' product.

Brazil emerged as the second-largest buyer (70–120 kbd), capitalising on steep Urals-linked pricing discounts, while North/West African destinations (Morocco, Ghana, Egypt) collectively absorbed 50–100 kbd — new trade routes that barely existed pre-2022.

The September 2025 trough of 586 kbd likely reflected OPEC+ voluntary production cuts reducing Russian refinery throughput, before recovering to 924 kbd in January 2026 as the Hormuz crisis created global product supply anxiety and premium-priced demand.

recent: Russian gasoline exports collapsed to zero after 9-15 Mar -- the single 38 kbd cargo to Libya was the last before Russia's April 1 gasoline export ban took effect. Moscow prioritised domestic supply security over export revenue, pre-emptively cutting exports weeks before the formal ban date.

This is structurally bullish for Mediterranean and African gasoline markets that had relied on Russian barrels, compounding the loss of Gulf refinery output from Bapco Sitra (405 kbd offline) and Kuwait Al-Ahmadi strikes.
quarterly: Russian gasoline exports were essentially zero throughout Q1 2026, with only three sporadic cargoes: Togo (39 kbd, 9–15 Feb), Libya (38 kbd, 2–8 Mar), and Libya again (38 kbd, 9–15 Mar) — confirming Russia's structural position as a gasoline-deficit producer.

Russia's formal ban on gasoline exports from 1 April cements what was already reality: domestic refinery capacity is fully absorbed by internal demand, and the Gulf crisis has only intensified the Kremlin's protectionist posture on light products.
yearly: Russian gasoline exports effectively ceased: volumes collapsed from 86 kbd in May 2025 to near-zero by October, with Russia formally banning gasoline exports from April 1, 2026 to secure domestic supply amid rising internal fuel prices and refinery maintenance cycles.

The few residual cargoes (17 kbd to Libya in March) represent legacy contract fulfilment rather than commercial trade — Russia has structurally exited the global gasoline export market for the foreseeable future.

recent: Russian naphtha exports declined steadily from 677 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~426-428 kbd (23-29 Mar and partial 30 Mar-4 Apr), a 37% drop. China and India remain the primary buyers but at lower volumes -- China fell from 231 to 73 kbd while India fell from 155 to 51 kbd.

Saudi Arabia's purchase of 71 kbd of Russian naphtha (9-15 Mar) is notable -- the kingdom is importing naphtha for petrochemical feedstock as its own NGL processing is curtailed by offshore field closures, underscoring how deeply the crisis has disrupted even the Gulf's internal supply chains. Indonesia appeared at 115 kbd (16-22 Mar) as Southeast Asian crackers scramble for feedstock.
quarterly: Russian naphtha exports held broadly stable at ~512 kbd through Q1, essentially flat (+2%) versus pre-crisis levels — but the customer mix underwent a crisis-driven reshuffling as Asia competed for alternatives to halted Gulf naphtha.

China emerged as the dominant buyer at 191–231 kbd in early March (up from ~80–100 kbd in January), directly substituting for lost Saudi and UAE naphtha flows to feed Chinese crackers; India also intensified purchases at 155 kbd in 9–15 Mar.

An unusual Saudi Arabia cargo (71 kbd, 9–15 Mar) suggests reverse-flow naphtha reaching the Gulf — potentially for blending or domestic use as Saudi refinery throughput at Yanbu maxes out and inland petrochemical feedstock tightens.
yearly: Russian naphtha exports strengthened from 346 kbd in November 2025 to 504–522 kbd in December–January — a 50% surge — as Asian petrochemical crackers desperately sought non-Gulf feedstock, with Singapore, India, and China competing for Siberian-origin cargoes.

Gulf-destined naphtha swung wildly: 14% Gulf share in January 2026 (led by Oman's Sohar refinery at ~70 kbd) collapsed to 3.2% by March as Hormuz closure severed direct delivery routes, though IRGC-tolled passage at ~$2M/vessel in yuan kept some trickle flows moving.

Venezuela's emergence as a top-3 buyer (50–100 kbd in multiple months) reflects the deepening Russia-Venezuela energy axis, with naphtha serving as barter currency for Venezuelan crude oil in sanctions-evading bilateral trade.

recent: Russian sulphur exports remain minimal at 0.4-19 ktons/d, almost exclusively to Morocco (OCP phosphate operations). China appeared briefly at 11 ktons/d in 23-29 Mar. With Gulf sulphur from Qatar, UAE, and Saudi gas processing largely offline, the global sulphur market is tightening but Russia's export base is too small to fill the gap.
quarterly: Russian sulphur exports averaged ~9 ktons/day in Q1 2026, a small but steady flow dominated by Morocco's OCP phosphate complex (which absorbed ~28 ktons cumulatively during the crisis period).

China appeared as a new buyer (11 ktons cumulatively in March), potentially replacing lost Gulf-sourced sulphur as Saudi Aramco's and ADNOC's gas processing operations — major global sulphur sources — remain curtailed by the conflict.
yearly: Russian sulphur exports trended down from 15–18 ktons/d in mid-2025 to ~10 ktons/d by late 2025/early 2026, reflecting reduced Russian refinery throughput and competing Chinese domestic production — Morocco remains the dominant buyer at 4–7 ktons/d, feeding OCP Group's massive phosphate fertiliser complex.
With Gulf NGL-linked sulphur from Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Qatar offline since March, Russian sulphur pricing should command a structural premium even as volumes remain constrained.

recent: US crude exports hit ~4.6 mb/d in 23-29 Mar -- near all-time record levels -- before easing to ~3.5 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr. The buyer base expanded to 16 countries including first-time destinations Denmark (205 kbd), Australia (86 kbd), and China (109 kbd), confirming BIC Magazine and EIA reports of unprecedented diversification.

Asia is the dominant pull: South Korea led at 695-807 kbd, Japan surged to 580 kbd (23-29 Mar), and Taiwan held at 196-331 kbd. The Netherlands (466-716 kbd) remains the key European hub, while Spain appeared at 410 kbd -- a new major buyer as Mediterranean refiners replace lost Gulf crude.

With US production at 13.6 mb/d and SPR releases ongoing (45.2 of 172 mb delivered), the US has become the global swing supplier. However, Gulf Coast port capacity is the binding constraint -- logistics bottlenecks will cap further upside even as Brent trades above $110.
quarterly: US crude exports averaged ~3.92 mb/d across the crisis period, broadly flat versus the pre-crisis Q1 average of 3.89 mb/d — but peaked at 4.59 mb/d in 23–29 Mar, consistent with reports of US exports surging toward record levels with buyer bases expanding to 15+ countries.

The customer mix has reshaped toward crisis-driven demand: Japan appeared as a top-3 buyer at 580–581 kbd in mid-to-late March (negligible pre-crisis), South Korea intensified to 688–807 kbd, and the UK emerged at 356–400+ kbd — all traditionally Gulf-dependent crude importers now pulling US barrels.

The 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr showed a pullback to 3.49 mb/d, likely reflecting cargo scheduling rather than demand softening; Taiwan's re-emergence at 332 kbd signals continued Asian pull for WTI-linked crudes.

European uptake is structural, not temporary: Netherlands (averaging ~440 kbd) and France (~400 kbd) are establishing US crude as a baseline feedstock, not a crisis substitute — a reshaping of Atlantic Basin trade flows that may persist post-conflict.
yearly: US crude exports averaged 3.8 mb/d over 12 months, peaking at 4.28 mb/d in September 2025 before the Hormuz crisis drove a second surge: March 2026 hit 3.94 mb/d with buyer diversification reaching unprecedented levels — exports now flow to 15+ countries including first-time destinations Denmark, Spain, and Australia per BIC Magazine reporting.

Japan's emergence as a top-3 buyer in March 2026 is the clearest structural signal of the crisis: with 93.5% ME crude dependency, Japanese refiners are urgently securing WTI Midland cargoes for the first time at scale — a trade route that would have been uneconomic pre-crisis but now works at $110+ Brent.

South Korea and Netherlands remain the steady anchor buyers (600–900 kbd combined), but the SPR release of 45.2 mb already delivered (of 172 mb authorised) is supplementing commercial flows — the IEA is weighing a second coordinated release if Hormuz remains shut past May.

recent: US LNG exports held remarkably steady at 368-388 ktons/d (~134-142 Mt/yr annualised) across all four weeks, confirming that all 8 US terminals are running at or above nameplate capacity. Cheniere's Sabine Pass at 110% utilisation is the new global LNG kingpin with Qatar's 77 Mtpa fully offline.

Destination diversification surged: Egypt emerged as a major buyer (32-46 ktons/d) as Chevron's Leviathan FM and Qatar's LNG halt cut the Mediterranean's two primary gas sources simultaneously. Thailand (11-34 ktons/d), Bangladesh (9-10 ktons/d), and India (12-20 ktons/d) appeared as Asia scrambles for replacement cargoes.

Europe still commands the largest share (France, Netherlands, Germany, Spain, Italy, Belgium collectively ~120-180 ktons/d) but the margin is narrowing as Asian spot LNG at $20+/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis) pulls cargoes eastward. Golden Pass LNG (QatarEnergy 70%/ExxonMobil 30%) expected to add its first export cargo in April, providing incremental supply.
quarterly: US LNG exports climbed steadily through Q1 to average ~371 ktons/day during the crisis period (+6% vs pre-crisis), with a clear uptrend: 354 ktons/day in early March → 388 ktons/day in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr — consistent with Cheniere and all 8 US terminals running at maximum capacity (~14.6 Bcf/d total).

Europe dominates the buyer mix: France (206 ktons cumulative crisis), Netherlands (191 ktons), Italy (145 ktons), and Germany (112 ktons) collectively absorb the majority, as European utilities lock in US LNG to replace both Qatari (FM'd) and soon-to-be-banned Russian volumes.

Asia remains underserved despite record spot prices ($20+ JKM): Japan (57 ktons cumulative crisis) and Thailand (34 ktons in 23–29 Mar) are marginal US LNG buyers — the infrastructure simply doesn't exist to redirect US Gulf Coast cargoes to the Pacific at the speed Asian markets need, leaving Qatari customers stranded.

Golden Pass LNG (QatarEnergy 70%/ExxonMobil 30%) ramping feed gas to 434 MMcf/d in Texas offers the first meaningful US-based LNG capacity expansion — but its first cargo isn't expected until April, providing no near-term relief.
yearly: US LNG exports surged 44% from the June 2025 trough (260 ktons/d) to a record 373 ktons/d in March 2026, with Cheniere now the world's largest LNG exporter and Sabine Pass running at 110% nameplate utilisation — all 8 US terminals are at maximum output per EIA data.

The buyer mix shifted dramatically: Turkey and the UK surged into the top-3 from November 2025 onwards (replacing traditional Netherlands/France dominance), while Egypt emerged as a top-5 buyer at 20–30 ktons/d as Israeli Leviathan gas exports to Cairo remained halted under Chevron's FM.

With Qatar's ~20% of global LNG supply offline and Asian JKM spot at $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis forecasts), US LNG is commanding historic premiums — but physical export capacity is maxed at ~14.6 Bcf/d with no new trains until 2028, meaning the supply gap cannot be closed from US sources alone.

recent: US LPG exports averaged ~148-165 ktons/d across March, with Japan the dominant buyer at 28-68 ktons/d (highly variable cargo-by-cargo). China's offtake doubled from 7 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to 23 ktons/d (30 Mar-4 Apr) as the world's largest LPG importer scrambles for non-Gulf supply following Saudi, UAE, and Qatar NGL shutdowns.

Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals are benefiting from an expanding LPG-naphtha spread that favours US propane as petrochemical feedstock. Indonesia (5-14 ktons/d), South Korea (~6-8 ktons/d), and Vietnam (6 ktons/d) are all growing customers, while the Latin American base (Mexico, Dominican Republic, Honduras, Colombia) provides steady baseline demand.
quarterly: US LPG exports held steady at ~149 ktons/day through Q1, up 3% from pre-crisis levels — a modest gain given the massive Middle East NGL supply gap from Saudi, UAE, and Qatari production shutdowns.

Japan remains the anchor buyer at 27–68 ktons/day, with a surge to 68 ktons/day in 23–29 Mar suggesting Japanese importers building strategic stocks; China's share has notably declined (60 ktons cumulative crisis vs 194 ktons pre-crisis), possibly reflecting Chinese PDH plants drawing on domestic stockpiles rather than importing at spot premiums.

Enterprise and Targa's Gulf Coast terminal throughput appears near capacity — the inability to grow US LPG exports by more than 3% despite the favourable LPG-naphtha spread and massive Asian demand signals infrastructure constraints, not lack of market.
yearly: US LPG exports averaged 140 ktons/d over 12 months, with Japan, China, and South Korea collectively absorbing 80–90% of volumes — a remarkably stable trade despite crisis turbulence, reflecting the structural lock-in of US Gulf Coast Enterprise/Targa terminal contracts.

The April partial shows a jump to 174 ktons/d with China leading — the first evidence of post-March demand acceleration as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL-linked LPG supply (normally ~30% of Asian imports) has been completely offline since early March, forcing PDH plant operators to pay crisis premiums for US cargoes.

LPG-naphtha spread dynamics are now favouring increased US exports as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked constraints, but US export terminal capacity is the binding constraint — unlike LNG, LPG infrastructure cannot surge above 100% for extended periods.

recent: US jet fuel exports surged to 334 kbd in 16-22 Mar before stabilising at ~322 kbd (23-29 Mar) and ~236 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr.
