The UK appeared as a major new buyer at 84-134 kbd (normally a net exporter of jet fuel), reflecting Europe's acute aviation fuel deficit as Gulf middle distillate supply vanishes.

New intercontinental routes are emerging: Australia (43 kbd, 16-22 Mar), the Netherlands (44-51 kbd), and a widening Latin American base (Mexico, Peru, Ecuador, Colombia) signal that US Gulf Coast refiners running at max throughput are the global jet fuel backstop.
quarterly: US jet fuel exports rose ~6% to an average of ~252 kbd across the crisis period, with a dramatic geographic shift: the UK surged to become the #1 destination at 84–134 kbd in mid-to-late March (vs. minimal pre-crisis), consistent with UK refineries struggling to replace Gulf-sourced kerosene.

The Netherlands also emerged as a major new buyer (44–51 kbd in late March), signalling ARA hub jet fuel stockbuilding — European aviation fuel markets are drawing transatlantic barrels at premium pricing as Middle East product supply remains offline.

Australia appeared as a US jet fuel destination for the first time (43 kbd in 16–22 Mar), underscoring the extreme measures Gulf-dependent markets are taking to secure alternative supplies at prices well above pre-crisis norms.
yearly: US jet fuel exports grew steadily from 150 kbd in May 2025 to 262 kbd in March 2026 — a 74% increase — driven by the UK's insatiable demand (jumping from near-zero to 60–90 kbd as Gulf-sourced kerosene to Heathrow/Gatwick dried up).

Mexico and Canada remain the structural floor at 40–80 kbd combined (pipeline/cross-border), but the crisis has added a transatlantic dimension: Netherlands appeared in March's top-3 for the first time, signalling ARA jet fuel stocks are being replenished from US Gulf Coast sources rather than traditional Middle Eastern suppliers.

The 74% YoY growth trajectory positions US jet exports as a permanent structural shift if the Hormuz closure persists — European aviation fuel security has been fundamentally re-routed toward the US Gulf Coast.

recent: US diesel exports swung sharply -- from 1.19 mb/d (9-15 Mar) down to 0.95 mb/d (16-22 Mar), then surging to 1.72 mb/d (23-29 Mar) before settling at ~1.41 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr. The W13 spike represents the highest weekly US diesel export rate in the crisis period, with barrels reaching 19 countries.

Destination geography has gone global: the Netherlands surged to 235 kbd (23-29 Mar), South Africa held at 43-128 kbd, Chile at 57-148 kbd, and new African buyers (Morocco, Togo, Guatemala) reflect the worldwide diesel scramble. US Gulf Coast refiners -- Valero, Marathon, Phillips 66 -- are running strongest margins in years per BIC Magazine, and maximising diesel output.

The pattern of volatile weekly swings reflects cargo scheduling lumpiness rather than demand instability -- the underlying trend is clearly upward as the US becomes supplier-of-last-resort for Atlantic Basin and Southern Hemisphere diesel markets.
quarterly: US diesel/gasoil exports surged 15% to an average of ~1.23 mb/d across the crisis period, spiking to 1.72 mb/d in 23–29 Mar — the highest weekly rate in Q1 — as Gulf Coast refiners maximised throughput to fill the global middle distillate gap left by shuttered Gulf refineries (Bapco Sitra, Kuwait's Al-Ahmadi).

Destination diversification is dramatic: South Africa (399 kbd cumulative crisis), Netherlands (408 kbd), and Peru (407 kbd) all surged into the top 5, alongside traditional buyers Chile and the UK — this is a structural rebalancing of global diesel trade flows.

The ramp from 900 kbd in early March to 1.72 mb/d by late March represents a near-doubling in under 4 weeks, consistent with Gulf Coast refineries restarting and maximising distillate output at $110+ Brent margins.
yearly: US diesel exports averaged 1,150 kbd over 12 months with a clear crisis-driven surge: April 2026 partial is tracking at 1,350 kbd daily — the highest rate in the entire dataset — as Middle Eastern product supply to Europe and Africa collapsed.

The buyer mix underwent a structural reorientation in March 2026: Peru and Chile surged to the top-2 (from typical mid-table), while the UK and France entered the top-5 for the first time in the yearly window — transatlantic diesel trade that would normally be uneconomic is now viable at >$100 crack spreads.

Latin America (Mexico, Brazil, Chile, Peru) consistently absorbs 400–600 kbd — half of all US diesel exports — and this baseline has held steady through the crisis, meaning the European/African demand surge is genuinely incremental volume, not redirection.

recent: US gasoline exports held stable at ~784-854 kbd through March, anchored by Mexico (135-311 kbd) and a broad Latin American base (Colombia, Guatemala, Honduras, Ecuador, Costa Rica). Mexico's share declined from 311 kbd (9-15 Mar) to ~158 kbd (23-29 Mar), suggesting some demand destruction as fuel prices surge.

Non-traditional buyers appeared: Japan (46 kbd, 23-29 Mar) and Australia (87 kbd, 16-22 Mar) are both normally self-sufficient in gasoline, indicating spillover effects from naphtha feedstock shortages disrupting Asian refinery operations. Nigeria took 79-184 kbd across two weeks, filling the West African supply gap left by collapsed Gulf product exports.
quarterly: US gasoline exports held broadly flat at ~798 kbd during the crisis period, essentially unchanged from the pre-crisis Q1 average of 822 kbd — suggesting US refiners are channelling incremental crude throughput into higher-margin distillate and jet fuel rather than gasoline.

Mexico remains the dominant buyer at ~1.13 mb/d cumulatively, though its share is declining as Latin American destinations (Colombia, Guatemala, Ecuador, Honduras) absorb increasing volumes — the Western Hemisphere gasoline trade appears largely unaffected by the Gulf crisis.

The absence of meaningful crisis-driven gasoline export growth, despite Europe's loss of Saudi gasoline shipments, confirms US Gulf Coast refineries are prioritising diesel/gasoil (up 15%) and naphtha (up 65%) over gasoline in their product slate optimisation.
yearly: US gasoline exports averaged 770 kbd over 12 months with Mexico as the immovable anchor at 200–350 kbd monthly — reflecting structural pipeline and truck-based cross-border trade that operates independently of maritime crisis dynamics.

November 2025's spike to 1,011 kbd — the 12-month peak — coincided with Latin American peak driving season and Nigerian import surges, but March 2026's 859 kbd during the Hormuz crisis suggests US refiners are prioritising domestic supply and higher-margin diesel/jet over gasoline exports.

Colombia, Guatemala, and Ecuador collectively take 80–150 kbd as steady Caribbean/Central American buyers — these flows are insulated from the Gulf crisis and represent the stable structural floor of US gasoline trade.

recent: US naphtha exports nearly doubled from 419 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 759 kbd (16-22 Mar) as South Korea grabbed 251 kbd -- the largest single-country naphtha cargo of the month -- consistent with YNCC and other Korean crackers invoking FM on Middle East naphtha and pivoting to US supply. Japan remained a consistent buyer at 83-207 kbd.

Venezuela's steady 67-113 kbd offtake across all four weeks reflects the US sanctions waiver framework allowing naphtha-for-crude swaps that keep Venezuelan refineries running. The sharp decline to 224 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr likely reflects vessel scheduling gaps rather than a demand shift -- Asian naphtha prices remain at crisis premiums with Formosa FPCC at 50% capacity.
quarterly: US naphtha exports surged 65% to an average of ~470 kbd across the crisis period, the largest percentage increase of any US export commodity — peaking at 759 kbd in 16–22 Mar as Asian petrochemical crackers desperately sought alternatives to halted Gulf naphtha.

Japan emerged as a major buyer out of nowhere: 114 kbd (9–15 Mar), 83 kbd (16–22 Mar), and 207 kbd (23–29 Mar) — Japanese crackers that were 93.5% Middle East-dependent are now pulling transatlantic naphtha at enormous freight premiums, a trade route that barely existed pre-crisis.

South Korea spiked to 251 kbd in 16–22 Mar (from ~48–73 kbd pre-crisis), consistent with YNCC and other crackers declaring FM on ethylene but still running at reduced rates on whatever naphtha they can source — the LPG-naphtha spread favours any available feedstock.
Venezuela remains a consistent base-load supplier (100–220 kbd throughout Q1), suggesting US-Venezuelan naphtha trade has structural underpinnings beyond the current crisis.
yearly: US naphtha exports nearly doubled from the 2025 average (~280 kbd) to 506 kbd in March 2026 — the 12-month record — as Asian crackers scrambled for alternatives after Gulf naphtha supply collapsed and prices surged past $1,000/mt.

Venezuela's jump to #1 destination in March (estimated 150+ kbd) reflects intensifying bilateral trade under sanctions waivers, while Japan's first appearance as a top-3 buyer confirms the crisis-driven desperation: Japanese petrochemical firms are now sourcing US naphtha across the Pacific at unprecedented freight costs.

Brazil remains the steady structural buyer at 50–100 kbd monthly, feeding Braskem's ethylene crackers — but the March surge was driven entirely by new Asian and Venezuelan demand layered on top of this baseline.

recent: Australian crude exports fluctuated between 183-280 kbd across March, with South Korea the dominant buyer at 136-174 kbd every week -- a lifeline for Korean refiners that were 93%+ dependent on Middle East crude pre-crisis. Japan appeared in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr at 108 kbd, its first Australian crude purchases likely in years.

At ~0.2-0.3 mb/d, Australian crude exports are too small to meaningfully offset the 12-15 mb/d Gulf disruption, but the near-exclusive South Korea/Japan buyer mix signals how desperate Northeast Asian refiners are for non-Hormuz crude at any price -- Oman benchmark at $152-153/bbl for May underscores the premium these buyers are paying.
quarterly: Australian crude exports surged 43% to an average of ~252 kbd during the crisis period — modest in absolute terms but a significant relative shift for a producer that typically exports 175 kbd — as Asia-Pacific refiners redirected procurement away from the Gulf.

South Korea emerged as the dominant buyer, absorbing 613 kbd cumulatively during the crisis (vs. 357 kbd across 8 pre-crisis weeks), consistent with South Korea's record SPR release (22.46M bbl) and aggressive diversification away from its 93%+ Gulf crude dependency.

Japan appeared as a new Australian crude buyer at 108 kbd in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week — with 45 Japanese-entity ships stranded inside the Gulf, every non-Gulf crude source is being tapped regardless of grade mismatch or freight cost.

The ceiling is real: Australian crude production (~350 kbd total) limits export upside, and the 183–267 kbd crisis-period range likely represents near-maximum available for export after domestic refinery demand.
yearly: Australian crude exports reached a 12-month high of 246 kbd in March 2026, up 19% from the 2025 average of 183 kbd, as crisis-driven demand from South Korea (top buyer) and Singapore accelerated offtake of Woodside's and Santos's condensate-heavy grades.

Thailand's appearance as a top-3 buyer from March 2026 reflects Southeast Asia's active diversification away from Gulf dependency — Bangkok secured a bilateral Hormuz transit deal with Iran on March 28 but is simultaneously building Australian crude supply chains as a hedge.

The October 2025 anomaly (UAE at 27% gulf share) was a one-off Murban-grade swap via Singapore — Australia's structural export profile is entirely Asia-Pacific, with zero Hormuz exposure, making it one of the few major crude exporters completely insulated from the crisis chokepoint.

recent: Australian LNG exports dipped from 252 ktons/d (9-15 Mar) to 169 ktons/d (23-29 Mar) before recovering to 209 ktons/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr. Bloomberg reported Australia's energy sector posted a record Q1 as LNG exporters benefited from the Qatar supply gap, and all 10 operational plants (85.1 Mtpa capacity) are running at high utilisation.

Japan is the anchor buyer at 48-73 ktons/d, followed by China (38-50 ktons/d), South Korea (19-54 ktons/d), and Taiwan (11-53 ktons/d). The W13 dip to 169 ktons/d likely reflects maintenance windows or vessel scheduling constraints rather than demand weakness -- with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, Australian LNG commands significant Asian spot premiums and every available cargo has a buyer.

Darwin LNG restarted in January 2026 after the Barossa gas field launch, adding incremental supply. However, Australia's ~85 Mtpa can fill barely half the Qatar gap, keeping Asian spot LNG well above $20/MMBtu and forcing continued rationing across Japan, Korea, and Taiwan.
quarterly: Australian LNG exports dipped 6% to ~213 ktons/day during the crisis period, a counterintuitive decline given Qatar's 77 Mtpa is fully offline and Asian spot prices have surged to $20+ JKM — suggesting Australian terminals are physically maxed out and cannot ramp beyond nameplate capacity.

Japan remains the anchor at 71–83 ktons/day, with China maintaining 40–58 ktons/day — but the volumes are remarkably stable week-to-week, confirming these are long-term contract flows, not spot cargoes responding to price signals.

The dip to 169 ktons/day in 23–29 Mar likely reflects scheduled maintenance or cargo timing, not demand destruction; the recovery to 209 ktons/day in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr supports this interpretation.

Australia's inability to meaningfully increase LNG exports — despite commanding pricing power — underscores the structural supply gap: with Qatar offline, Yamal maxed out, and US LNG flowing primarily to Europe, the ~20% global LNG supply hole has no near-term solution.
yearly: Australian LNG exports remained remarkably stable at 198–229 ktons/d throughout the 12-month period — a testament to long-term contract structures (85%+ of volume) that buffer against spot market volatility, even as Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown removed ~20% of global LNG supply.

Japan absorbs 80–100 ktons/d consistently (40–45% share), with China at 50–70 ktons/d and South Korea at 20–40 ktons/d — these three buyers account for nearly all Australian LNG volume and have remained stable despite the crisis, reflecting term-contract security of supply that spot-dependent European buyers lack.

Cyclone disruptions to North West Shelf and Wheatstone reduced potential output gains, but the Australia-Japan 'quid pro quo' — increased LNG commitments in exchange for guaranteed refined fuel supply — positions Australia as the indispensable energy partner for Northeast Asian import security in the post-Hormuz world.

recent: Australian naphtha exports are minimal and sporadic -- a single ~49 kbd cargo to Singapore in alternating weeks (9-15 Mar and 23-29 Mar), zero in the other two weeks. Limited naphtha export infrastructure and the fact that Australia's condensate-heavy production is largely retained for domestic or regional refining explains the negligible volumes.
quarterly: Australian naphtha exports are negligible and sporadic — only two cargoes totalling ~49 kbd each in 9–15 Mar and 23–29 Mar, both destined for Singapore, interspersed with zero-volume weeks.
Australia is not a meaningful naphtha exporter and these volumes are too small to register in the global feedstock rebalancing driven by the Gulf crisis.
yearly: Australian naphtha exports are small and episodic (0–58 kbd), reflecting limited domestic refinery surplus. February 2026's spike to 58 kbd — the 12-month peak — coincided with peak Asian cracker demand for non-Gulf feedstock, but volumes are structurally too small to meaningfully offset the loss of Gulf naphtha supply to Asian petrochemical hubs.

recent: China's seaborne crude imports plunged from ~11.2 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to ~7.8 mb/d (23-29 Mar) as the Hormuz closure severed Gulf supply lines, before ticking up to ~8.4 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr -- Gulf share collapsed from 44% to just 13.5%, with Saudi Arabia and Iraq effectively disappearing from the top-5 supplier list by late March.

Russia cemented its dominance at 2.2-2.5 mb/d throughout, accounting for ~30% of total intake; Brazil surged from ~535 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 1,514 kbd (30 Mar-4 Apr) as teapot refiners and majors pivoted aggressively to Atlantic Basin barrels, with Angola, Canada, and even Panama/Argentina emerging as gap-fillers.

Pipeline flows (ESPO ~600 kbd, Kazakhstan ~220 kbd, Myanmar ~200 kbd) provided a ~1 mb/d floor immune to Hormuz disruption, but Beijing's Jan-Feb stockpiling at 12 mb/d now looks prescient -- the ~3.4 mb/d drop in seaborne receipts is testing commercial inventories and SPR buffers built during the pre-crisis buying spree.

Iraq collapsed from 781 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 73 kbd (23-29 Mar) despite Iran's Apr 4 declaration exempting Iraqi ships from Hormuz restrictions -- a signal that the exemption is not yet translating into actual loadings at Basra, where FM shut foreign-operated fields to 900 kbd from 3.3 mb/d.
quarterly: China's crude imports contracted sharply across Q1 2026, falling from a pre-crisis average of ~12.5 mb/d in January to just 7.8 mb/d in 23–29 Mar and 8.4 mb/d in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week — a 33% decline from January peaks as the Hormuz closure choked Gulf flows.

Russia cemented itself as China's indispensable supplier throughout the quarter, holding steady at 2.2–3.1 mb/d (pipeline + seaborne) and rising from ~22% share in early January to 30% by late March. Gulf share collapsed from 42–50% in January to just 13.5% in the partial week, with Saudi Arabia vanishing entirely in 23–29 Mar before reappearing at a token 332 kbd.

Iran continued supplying China at 550–1,400 kbd throughout March, consistent with reports that Iran allows Chinese-flagged vessels through Hormuz. But traditional Gulf suppliers — Iraq (FM declared Mar 20, collapsed from 1–2 mb/d to 73 kbd in W13), Kuwait (KPC FM, drone strikes), and UAE — effectively dropped out.

The supplier base diversified aggressively westward: Brazil (1.0–1.7 mb/d), Angola (0.2–0.8 mb/d), Canada, and Ecuador all surged as China scrambled for non-Gulf barrels, per Vortexa tracking. Pipeline flows from Kazakhstan (220 kbd) and Myanmar (200 kbd) provided a steady floor.
yearly: China's crude imports ran at ~11.7 mb/d through H2 2025, then surged to 12.2 mb/d in Jan-Feb 2026 as refiners pre-stocked ahead of the Hormuz crisis — Bloomberg reported a 15.8% YoY jump in early 2026 as Beijing directed strategic reserve fills.

March 2026 saw the first contraction to ~11.0 mb/d, but the real cliff arrived in April: the 4-day partial is running at just 8.2 mb/d — a 30% collapse from the Jan-Feb peak — as Gulf barrels vanished (Gulf share cratered from 44% avg to 10.5%).

Russia has become China's indispensable supplier, surging from ~1.87 mb/d in H2 2025 to 2.4 mb/d in March via ESPO pipeline and seaborne routes — now providing 22% of all imports, nearly double Saudi Arabia's share.

The Gulf supplier mix is fracturing: Iraq fell from 1.1-1.4 mb/d baseline to 966 kbd in March (consistent with Basra FM and 76% output collapse), while Kuwait virtually disappeared at 72 kbd vs the 250-520 kbd pre-crisis range.

recent: China's LNG imports fell sharply from ~134-140 kt/d in early-mid March to 82 kt/d (23-29 Mar) as Qatar -- previously supplying 45 kt/d -- vanished entirely following QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24; running at ~102 kt/d in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr, but with zero Qatari cargoes.

Australia stepped in as anchor supplier at 35-37 kt/d, with Malaysia, PNG, Russia, and a first-ever Canadian cargo (12 kt/d in 30 Mar-4 Apr) diversifying the mix; Oman LNG (outside Hormuz) appeared at 12.8 kt/d in early April, commanding significant spot premiums as the only Gulf-origin source still flowing.

The ~40% supply gap versus early March underscores the structural vulnerability flagged by Wanhua Chemical's FM on Middle East supplies -- pipeline gas via Power of Siberia provides partial insulation, but spot LNG costs have surged ~39% and further tightening is expected through mid-May.
quarterly: Qatar's LNG to China evaporated across Q1: from 48–98 ktons/d in January to 30–52 ktons/d in early March, then zero by 23–29 Mar — directly following QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24. Gulf share collapsed from 27–53% to 0% in a single week.

Total Chinese LNG imports halved from a Q1 average of ~180 ktons/d to 82–102 ktons/d in late March/early April. Australia became the anchor supplier at 35–37 ktons/d, while Russia (9–23 ktons/d), Malaysia, PNG, and Canada filled gaps — but volumes remain well below pre-crisis.

The trajectory shows a structural shift: Oman reappeared at 12.8 ktons/d in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week (first post-Hormuz Omani LNG transit via the new Iran-Oman coastal route protocol), potentially signaling a limited Gulf LNG pathway outside the blockade.
yearly: China's LNG imports collapsed from a seasonal peak of 247 ktons/d in December 2025 to just 124 ktons/d in March 2026 — a 50% structural decline driven by QatarEnergy's FM wiping out ~30% of China's Gulf-sourced supply.

The April partial (79 ktons/d over 4 days) shows zero Gulf LNG for the first time in the 12-month series, with Russia (34.6 ktons/d) overtaking Australia (18.2 ktons/d) as the top supplier — a dramatic reversal of the pre-crisis order where Qatar and Australia dominated.

Qatar's share eroded from ~55 ktons/d in Q4 2025 to 31.6 ktons/d in March as damaged Ras Laffan trains (12.8 Mtpa offline for 3-5 years) structurally removed supply; Wanhua Chemical's FM on Middle East feedstock underscores the downstream cascading impact.

recent: US Gulf Coast LPG has become China's lifeline, rising from 26 kt/d (9-15 Mar) to 30 kt/d (23-29 Mar) and holding at 30 kt/d in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr, now supplying ~74% of total intake as Saudi, Qatari, and Kuwaiti NGL-linked barrels dried up.

Iran continued LPG deliveries via IRGC-tolled Hormuz passage (charging ~$2M/vessel in yuan), fluctuating between 5-22 kt/d, but total imports fell from 59 to 41 kt/d in the latest partial week -- PDH plant operators are scrambling for alternative feedstock at sharply higher prices as the world's largest LPG importer faces a structural supply crunch.
quarterly: China — the world's largest LPG importer — saw Gulf share plunge from 45–68% in January (dominated by Iran, Qatar, UAE) to just 17% in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week, as ME NGL production collapsed alongside crude shutdowns.

The US emerged as the dominant replacement at 26–41 ktons/d by March (up from 3–17 ktons/d in January), with Canada also ramping. Iran continued exporting via IRGC-tolled Hormuz transit at 5–22 ktons/d — the only Gulf-linked LPG still reaching China. PDH plant operators are scrambling for cargoes at sharply higher prices, per ICIS.
yearly: China — the world's largest LPG importer — saw daily imports fall from ~68 ktons/d in H2 2025 to 60.5 in March and a concerning 36.3 in the April partial, as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL-linked supply collapsed alongside crude production shutdowns.

The US surged to fill the gap, rising from ~20-25 ktons/d pre-crisis to 28 ktons/d in March (46% share), while Iran maintained ~13 ktons/d via IRGC-tolled Hormuz passages — the two non-traditional dominant suppliers now account for 68% of China's LPG inflows.

Gulf share halved from 44% in H2 2025 to 29.5% in March; PDH plant operators are scrambling for US and Australian cargoes at sharply higher prices, with the LPG-naphtha spread compressing as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked constraints.

recent: Jet fuel imports effectively ceased after a single 24 kbd South Korean cargo in 9-15 Mar, with zero arrivals across the subsequent three weeks -- China's domestic refining capacity appears sufficient to meet aviation demand, and the commodity carries no Gulf exposure.
quarterly: China's jet/kerosene imports are negligible and entirely intra-Asian (South Korea), with zero Gulf dependency. Sporadic cargoes of 12–49 kbd appeared in only 4 of the 13 weeks, indicating China is essentially self-sufficient in jet fuel.
yearly: China's jet fuel imports — exclusively sourced from South Korea — are a negligible 2-17 kbd with zero Gulf exposure, providing full insulation from Hormuz disruption on this commodity.

recent: Diesel/gasoil imports remained minimal at 8-15 kbd, sourced exclusively from South Korea (with one Japanese cargo in W11) -- volumes are negligible relative to China's ~3.5 mb/d domestic refining output and reflect intra-Asian product balancing rather than a crisis-driven shift.
quarterly: China's diesel imports are minimal (mostly South Korean refined product at 8–74 kbd) with zero Gulf exposure. Russia appeared in some weeks. Volumes declined from ~55 kbd in January to ~8–12 kbd by late March, likely reflecting lower industrial activity and slowing trucking demand as the oil price shock suppressed economic output.
yearly: China's gasoil/diesel imports remain marginal at 12-48 kbd, entirely sourced from regional refiners (South Korea, Japan, Russia) with zero Gulf dependence — the real diesel risk sits in China's domestic refinery run cuts as crude supply tightens.

recent: Gulf naphtha supply to China collapsed entirely by 23-29 Mar (0% Gulf share, down from 44% in 9-15 Mar), with total imports plummeting 64% from 425 kbd to 153 kbd in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr -- Kuwait (124 kbd in W11) and Qatar disappeared completely as feedstock FM declarations cascaded through the supply chain.

Russia became the only consistent supplier at 63-106 kbd, supplemented by India (90 kbd) in early April; but the gap is enormous -- this naphtha shortfall is feeding directly into the ~5% global ethylene capacity shutdown across Northeast Asia, with cracker utilisation falling from 80% to 60% in March per industry data.

The supply vacuum validates Formosa Plastics' FM on PVC/HDPE and YNCC's ethylene halt -- naphtha has breached $1,000/mt in Singapore, a 60% surge that makes cracker economics untenable even where feedstock is physically available.
quarterly: China's naphtha imports collapsed from a pre-crisis average of ~425 kbd (with Gulf share peaking at 71% when Bahrain, UAE, Kuwait, and Qatar were flowing) to just 153 kbd in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week, with Gulf share at 0% — a complete supply-source inversion.

Gulf naphtha was still arriving at 170–324 kbd through mid-March (cargoes loaded pre-crisis), but by 23–29 Mar the last Gulf barrels had cleared. Russia surged to become the primary source at 63–106 kbd, joined by Norway, Thailand, South Korea, and India as emergency non-Gulf alternatives.

The naphtha shock directly feeds Wanhua Chemical's FM on isocyanates and the broader Asian petrochemical crisis — crackers operating at ~60% utilization in March as 60–70% of Asian naphtha normally transits Hormuz, per TankTerminals.
yearly: China's naphtha imports — critical petrochemical feedstock — collapsed from 508 kbd in February (pre-crisis stockpiling with Kuwait at 85 kbd and UAE at 82 kbd) to 312 kbd in March and just 197 kbd in the April partial, a 61% decline that is crippling cracker economics.

Gulf naphtha share was a commanding 54% in Jan-Feb 2026 as Chinese buyers front-loaded ME cargoes; by April, Gulf supply hit zero — forcing total dependence on India (136 kbd) and Russia (62 kbd), neither of which can scale fast enough to replace ~270 kbd of lost Gulf volumes.

This structural shortage is cascading through Asia's petrochemical chain: Formosa Plastics (Taiwan), YNCC (South Korea), and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) have all declared FM citing naphtha feedstock loss, with Asia facing ~1 million metric tons of ethylene production losses in April per industry estimates.

recent: India's crude imports stabilised at 4.2-4.6 mb/d (23 Mar-4 Apr) after an initial contraction to 3.6 mb/d (9-15 Mar), with Russia dominant at 1.6-2.5 mb/d (38-62% share) as the US sanctions waiver (Mar 5-Apr 11) enabled aggressive purchasing -- over 30M barrels in a single week per OilPrice.

Gulf crude arrivals swung wildly from 5.8% (9-15 Mar) to 35.6% (23-29 Mar) and back to 19% (30 Mar-4 Apr), suggesting intermittent convoy-escorted deliveries under Operation Urja Suraksha rather than steady flow; Saudi Yanbu-routed barrels appeared consistently at 157-952 kbd, confirming the East-West Pipeline bypass is partially working for India.

New suppliers emerged rapidly: Nigeria (413 kbd), Ecuador (302 kbd), Brazil (457 kbd), and Colombia (131-285 kbd) are all filling gaps that didn't exist pre-crisis, while MRPL's FM cutting 500 kbd of refinery runs constrains the ability to process additional crude even if it arrives.
quarterly: India's crude imports contracted ~18% from a pre-crisis average of ~5.1 mb/d (Jan–Feb) to 3.6–4.6 mb/d across March, with the sharpest single-week drop to 3.58 mb/d in 9–15 Mar when Gulf share collapsed to just 5.8% as Hormuz-transiting cargoes dried up.

Russia became India's dominant crude supplier, surging from ~1.0–1.4 mb/d in January to 1.9–2.6 mb/d in March — a near-doubling consistent with Business Standard's reporting of 94% MoM increase to 2.06 mb/d. Russian share reached 55% in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week, up from ~22% pre-crisis.

Iraq virtually disappeared after the Mar 20 FM declaration (from 1.0–1.4 mb/d to 49–61 kbd). Saudi Arabia and UAE arrivals became highly sporadic. Angola, Colombia, Nigeria, US, and Brazil collectively filled some of the gap, but India's ~25-day crude/fuel stock buffer is being drawn down rapidly.

Gulf share swung wildly week-to-week (5.8% to 35.6% to 19%), suggesting India is receiving sporadic cargoes — likely under the Indian Navy's Operation Urja Suraksha convoy system rather than normal commercial transit.
yearly: India's crude imports followed a classic crisis arc: stockpiling to ~5.2 mb/d in Jan-Feb 2026 (up 8% from the H2 2025 avg of 4.8 mb/d), then contracting to 4.4 mb/d in March as Hormuz closure disrupted ~60% of India's traditional Gulf supply chain.

Russia has become India's dominant crude supplier at 2.06 mb/d in March — up 26% from the H2 2025 average of 1.63 mb/d — as New Delhi leveraged the US sanctions waiver (Mar 5-Apr 11) and the Puri-Sorokin agreement to secure surge volumes. Russia now provides 46% of Indian crude imports.

Gulf share plummeted from 54.5% in Jan-Feb to 26.4% in March: Iraq dropped from ~1 mb/d to 235 kbd (Basra FM), UAE from 500 kbd to 203 kbd (Habshan shutdowns), while Saudi volumes halved to 572 kbd with Yanbu pipeline maxed out.

The April partial at 3.1 mb/d (4-day read) is alarming — a 40% drop from the stockpiling peak — with India's ~25 days of crude/fuel stocks providing limited buffer before Operation Urja Suraksha escorted convoys become India's supply lifeline.

recent: India's LNG imports swung from 31-35 kt/d (9-22 Mar) to 73 kt/d (23-29 Mar) -- the surge driven by Omani cargoes (27 kt/d, outside Hormuz chokepoint) and Nigerian/US spot purchases filling the gap left by Qatar's total absence; running at 62 kt/d in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr.

Qatar has completely vanished from India's LNG supply despite historically providing 41% of imports; Petronet LNG's FM on its 7.5 Mtpa Dahej terminal contracts and Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cuts underscore the severity, though Russia offered to increase LNG supplies at the Apr 3 Puri-Sorokin meeting.
quarterly: Qatar's LNG to India vanished by early March, dropping from 27–51 ktons/d in January to zero — consistent with Petronet LNG's FM declaration and the 92% plunge in Qatar LNG imports reported by Business Standard. Gulf share fell from 55–75% to 0% in the 16–22 Mar week.

Oman emerged as India's only viable Gulf LNG source (17–33 ktons/d) due to its position outside the Hormuz chokepoint, while US (8–12 ktons/d), Nigeria, and Angola became critical alternatives. Total volumes fell ~40% from ~75 ktons/d pre-crisis to ~31–62 ktons/d in March.
The LNG shortfall is cascading into industry: Gujarat Gas imposed 50% industrial supply cuts, and India is negotiating emergency Russian LNG purchases following the Puri-Sorokin meeting on Mar 19.
yearly: India's LNG imports fell from a pre-crisis baseline of ~68-76 ktons/d to 52.7 ktons/d in March — a 25% structural decline driven by the near-total loss of Qatari supply (Qatar provided 41.4% of India's LNG pre-crisis, per Petronet LNG disclosures).

Gulf share collapsed from 67% in Jan-Feb to 42% in March as Qatar deliveries ceased; Oman (15.9 ktons/d) emerged as the top Gulf supplier leveraging its position outside the Hormuz chokepoint, while US spot cargoes (10.9 ktons/d) entered the mix for the first time.

Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cut and Petronet LNG's FM declaration reflect the severity — India is negotiating Russian LNG purchases following the Puri-Sorokin meeting, but pipeline gas from Power of Siberia is not an option for India unlike China.

recent: Indian Navy-escorted convoys are delivering results: LPG imports surged to 26.3 kt/d (30 Mar-4 Apr) from a low of 11.1 kt/d (16-22 Mar), with Gulf share rebounding to 66% as Saudi (7.6 kt/d), Qatar, and even Iran (4.2 kt/d) resumed deliveries -- consistent with the Pine Gas and Jag Vasant convoy arriving Indian ports in late March under Operation Urja Suraksha.

The US remains the non-Gulf anchor at 8-10 kt/d throughout; the W14 surge suggests the military escort model is viable for critical LPG supply, though sustaining it depends on Iran continuing to permit tolled passage for 'friendly nation' vessels.
quarterly: India's LPG imports — ~90% ME-dependent pre-crisis — collapsed from 26–43 ktons/d in January to just 11 ktons/d in 16–22 Mar, a 70% contraction consistent with the reported 45% March decline. With only ~2 weeks of LPG inventory, this is approaching a domestic cooking fuel emergency.

The US surged to become the #1 LPG supplier at 7–10 ktons/d in March (from 2–5 ktons/d pre-crisis), while Gulf sources shrank dramatically. The Indian Navy escorted LPG tankers Pine Gas and Jag Vasant through Hormuz under Operation Urja Suraksha, with the first convoy arriving Mar 26–28.

A notable development in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week: Iran appeared as a new LPG source (4.2 ktons/d), suggesting India may be tapping IRGC-tolled Hormuz passage, and Saudi volumes partially recovered to 7.6 ktons/d — potentially early signs of convoy-facilitated normalization.
yearly: India's LPG imports — 90% Gulf-dependent pre-crisis — plunged 39% from 33.7 ktons/d in H2 2025 to 20.7 ktons/d in March, the sharpest proportional decline of any Indian commodity flow, reflecting the near-total shutdown of Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL processing.

Indian Navy Operation Urja Suraksha escorted LPG tankers Pine Gas and Jag Vasant (92,000t combined) through Hormuz in late March, enabling some recovery to 27.1 ktons/d in the April partial — but this is military-dependent logistics, not a sustainable supply chain.

The US stepped in as the primary non-Gulf alternative (7.9 ktons/d in March, up from near zero), though Gulf suppliers still provide 60% of what's flowing — highlighting India's structural inability to quickly diversify away from ME LPG.

recent: Russia has become India's dominant naphtha supplier, surging from 53 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 181 kbd (30 Mar-4 Apr) -- more than tripling in four weeks as Gulf-sourced naphtha evaporated; Oman provides secondary supply at 34-46 kbd via its non-Hormuz export position.

Total naphtha imports doubled from 101 to 214 kbd over the period, suggesting Indian crackers are actively rebuilding feedstock inventories rather than cutting runs -- a stark contrast to Japan and South Korea's conservation mode, likely enabled by Russia's willingness to redirect product at discounted rates.
quarterly: India's naphtha procurement underwent a complete supplier transformation: from near-100% Gulf (UAE, Kuwait) in January to 84% Russian by the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week (180.5 kbd out of 214.4 kbd), with Oman providing the only Gulf-linked barrels at 34 kbd.

Russia was already emerging as a naphtha supplier in late January (147 kbd in W05), but the crisis accelerated the pivot — Russian naphtha now supplies virtually all of MRPL and other Indian cracker/refinery feedstock needs. UAE and Kuwait disappeared entirely as naphtha sources by mid-March, consistent with KPC FM and Gulf refinery shutdowns.
yearly: Counter-intuitively, India's naphtha imports surged 49% from the 87 kbd H2 2025 baseline to 130 kbd in March and 193 kbd in the April partial — India is pivoting from importing crude to importing semi-finished feedstock as domestic refinery throughput falls.

Russia dominates the surge: naphtha from Russian Federation jumped from 43 kbd pre-crisis to 68 kbd in March, as Indian refiners cutting crude runs need naphtha from external sources to keep petrochemical crackers running — MRPL's FM on gasoline exports confirms the refinery constraint.
This structural shift — less crude, more naphtha — may persist even post-crisis as Indian refiners reconfigure supply chains toward Russian intermediate products and away from Gulf crude dependency.

recent: Japan's crude imports halved from ~2.0 mb/d (9-22 Mar) to ~1.0-1.1 mb/d (23 Mar-4 Apr), the most severe contraction of any major Asian importer -- and with Gulf dependence still at 91-100% (Saudi 661, UAE 195 kbd in latest partial week), Tokyo has virtually no non-Middle East supply diversification.

Unlike India and China, Japan has not pivoted to Russian or Atlantic Basin crude: Brunei (92 kbd in 30 Mar-4 Apr) is the only non-Gulf origin to appear, while S&P Global reports 45 Japanese-entity ships remain stranded in the Gulf -- the 80M-barrel SPR release (54M crude + 26M products) announced via IEA is the primary buffer keeping refineries running.

The 50% import collapse validates Daiwa Institute's warning that even a 10% ME LNG import cut would push Japan GDP negative -- on the crude side, the damage is far worse, and April tanker arrivals are set to plummet further as the pipeline of in-transit cargoes from pre-crisis loadings exhausts.
quarterly: Japan — 93.5% ME-dependent for crude — is facing the most acute supply crisis among Asian importers. Imports plummeted from ~2.4 mb/d pre-crisis to just 1.09 mb/d in 23–29 Mar and 1.03 mb/d in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week, a 57% collapse.

Critically, Gulf share REMAINED at 91–100% even as volumes halved — Japan has NOT diversified, it simply received less oil. Saudi Arabia and UAE are still virtually the only suppliers, with negligible volumes from Ecuador, Malaysia, Brunei, and the US in scattered weeks. This reflects Japan's structural inability to quickly pivot to non-Gulf grades.

The 45 Japanese-entity ships stranded in the Gulf per S&P Global underscore the logistical paralysis. The MOL Sohar LNG transit on Apr 3 was celebrated as a milestone — the first Japan-linked vessel to cross Hormuz since the crisis began, signaling potential for limited resumption under bilateral protocols.
yearly: Japan's crude imports collapsed from 2.25 mb/d (H2 2025 avg) to 1.67 mb/d in March and just 1.20 mb/d in the April partial — a staggering 47% decline that reflects Japan's catastrophic 93.5% dependence on Middle Eastern crude with virtually no diversification alternatives.

Unlike China and India, Japan's Gulf share actually increased to 96.3% in March — Japan cannot pivot to Russia or Latin American crude at scale, leaving it entirely dependent on the trickle of Saudi barrels reaching Yanbu (Saudi Arabia: 903 kbd in March, down from ~1.4 mb/d baseline).

S&P Global reports April tanker arrivals set to plummet further, with 45 Japanese-entity ships stranded in the Gulf; the first Japan-linked LNG tanker (MOL's Sohar LNG) crossing Hormuz on April 3 was hailed as a milestone — underscoring how dire the shipping situation has become.

Japan's pre-crisis import profile shows zero structural preparation: the country did not stockpile in Jan-Feb 2026 (2.39 mb/d, barely above the 2.25 H2 avg), unlike China and India which both surged 5-10% ahead of the conflict.

recent: Japan's LNG imports declined steadily from 201 kt/d (9-15 Mar) to 154 kt/d (30 Mar-4 Apr), a 23% drop, but the mix has radically diversified -- Australia (24-111 kt/d), Malaysia (18-36 kt/d), PNG (10-26 kt/d), Canada, Brunei, and Indonesia all contributing, with Qatar down from 10.7 kt/d to zero by late March.

The supplier diversification reflects years of Japanese energy security planning (long-term Australian and Southeast Asian contracts) paying off under crisis conditions, but total volumes are still falling and JKM spot prices at $20.39/MMBtu are 2x pre-crisis forecasts -- Oman LNG (13 kt/d in W14) is the only Gulf-origin supply still flowing.

The first Japan-linked LNG tanker (Sohar LNG, MOL) crossing Hormuz on Apr 3 was a milestone, but one cargo does not restore the ~40 kt/d of Qatari/Gulf supply that has vanished -- Japan faces a widening gap as summer cooling demand approaches.
quarterly: Japan's LNG supply held up better than crude thanks to pre-crisis diversification — Qatar was only ~5–15% of supply, and Australia anchored imports at 24–111 ktons/d throughout Q1. Total volumes dipped only modestly from ~230 ktons/d to 154–167 ktons/d in late March.

Qatar disappeared entirely from Japan's LNG mix by late March, but the impact was muted by existing contracts with Australia (NWS, Ichthys, Wheatstone), Malaysia (MLNG), Russia (Sakhalin-2), PNG, Brunei, and Indonesia. Canada and Mozambique cargoes also appeared.

However, price is the weapon: JKM Asian spot surged to $20.39/MMBtu (2x pre-crisis), and Daiwa Institute warns a 10% ME LNG import cut would push Japan's GDP negative. Japan is paying enormously for supply security even if volumes are relatively stable.
yearly: Japan's LNG supply has proven remarkably resilient: imports held at 179 ktons/d in March and rebounded to 213 ktons/d in the April partial, close to the H2 2025 avg of 182 ktons/d — testament to Japan's well-diversified LNG portfolio with only ~5% Qatar exposure.

Australia (85 ktons/d in March) and Malaysia (27 ktons/d) anchor Japan's supply, both positioned outside the Hormuz chokepoint; however, JKM spot prices have doubled to $20.39/MMBtu from the pre-crisis $10 forecast, meaning Japan pays dearly for supply stability.

Daiwa Institute's warning that a 10% cut in ME LNG imports would push Japan's GDP negative explains Tokyo's aggressive spot procurement — with Cheniere now the world's largest LNG exporter at 110% nameplate utilisation, US cargoes are being bid away from European buyers.

recent: Japan has completed a full pivot to US LPG: the United States supplied 100% of imports across all four weeks (26-39 kt/d), with zero Gulf barrels and only a trace Australian cargo in W12 -- volumes are rising (32 kt/d in 23-29 Mar; 39 kt/d in partial 30 Mar-4 Apr), indicating Enterprise and Targa Gulf Coast terminals are absorbing displaced Asian demand.

The complete elimination of Gulf LPG (previously a major source) with rising US volumes suggests this trade lane restructuring may persist beyond the crisis, especially given the LPG-naphtha spread favouring US exports.
quarterly: Japan's LPG imports tell a remarkable energy security story: 100% sourced from the US throughout Q1 2026 (21–39 ktons/d), with zero Gulf exposure. This pre-crisis diversification means Japan has been almost completely insulated from the ME LPG supply collapse.
Volumes showed modest fluctuation (17–39 ktons/d) but no crisis-driven disruption, making Japan LPG the only major Asian commodity flow operating at near-normal levels throughout the Hormuz closure.
yearly: Japan completed a full pivot to US and Australian LPG over the 12-month period, with Gulf share at 0% throughout the crisis — US supply at 26-32 ktons/d is actually above the pre-crisis H2 2025 avg of 22 ktons/d, suggesting pre-positioned contracts.

While volumes are secure, the petrochemical feedstock calculus has shifted: with naphtha collapsing 53% and LPG holding steady, Japanese crackers may increasingly switch to LPG feedstock where flexibility exists, though at significantly higher prices.

recent: Jet fuel imports stabilised at 58-73 kbd (16 Mar-4 Apr) after a lower 31 kbd in 9-15 Mar, sourced entirely from South Korea -- reflecting Japan's refinery run cuts that have reduced domestic jet fuel output and forced reliance on Korean product surplus.

The steady South Korean supply suggests Seoul's refinery complex (despite its own crude supply challenges) is maintaining enough throughput to serve regional product demand, though Lotte Yeosu's halt could tighten this going forward.
quarterly: Japan's jet fuel/kerosene imports are entirely intra-Asian (South Korea and China), with zero Gulf dependency. Volumes have been volatile (10–234 kbd) but sourcing unaffected by the Hormuz crisis. South Korea dominates supply post-crisis, with China contributing in scattered weeks.
yearly: Japan's jet fuel imports surged from the 36 kbd H2 2025 average to 72 kbd in March — exclusively from South Korea and China — as domestic refinery run cuts forced Japan to compensate via product imports for the first time at this scale.

This near-doubling of jet fuel imports is a structural crisis indicator: Japan's refiners, starved of Gulf crude, are cutting runs rather than paying crisis premiums, while airlines still need fuel — creating a domestic supply-demand gap filled by regional refiners.

recent: Diesel imports were volatile at 15-62 kbd, primarily from South Korea with a one-off Brunei cargo (50 kbd in 16-22 Mar) -- the erratic pattern reflects spot purchasing as Japanese refiners cut crude throughput and domestic diesel output fluctuates week-to-week.
Zero Gulf diesel throughout the period; Japan is entirely dependent on intra-Asian product trade for any diesel import needs.
quarterly: Japan's diesel imports are minimal and entirely from regional sources (South Korea dominant, plus Brunei in one week), averaging under 20 kbd. Zero Gulf dependency. The commodity is too low-volume and regionally sourced to face meaningful Hormuz-related disruption.
yearly: Japan's gasoil/diesel imports spiked to 86 kbd in the April partial — more than triple the H2 2025 average of 26 kbd — signaling a potential emergency stockbuild as domestic refinery output falls with crude supply at half the pre-crisis level.
South Korea is the primary supplier (all product flows Gulf-free), but Seoul's own naphtha export ban and refinery pressures may constrain this lifeline if the Hormuz closure persists into Q2.

recent: Gasoline imports tripled from 27 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 85 kbd (23-29 Mar) before moderating to 67 kbd (30 Mar-4 Apr), almost entirely from South Korea; India appeared as a new supplier at 33 kbd in the partial week -- a first-ever gasoline flow reflecting MRPL and other Indian refiners redirecting product as domestic demand management kicks in.

The surging import dependency (previously Japan imported minimal gasoline) confirms that refinery run cuts are now feeding through into product availability, with the government's price cap at 170 yen/litre masking the true supply cost.
quarterly: Japan's gasoline imports — sourced entirely from South Korea (and India in W14) — actually increased in late March to 85 kbd in 23–29 Mar, likely as domestic refinery throughput declined due to crude supply shortages and Japan turned to product imports to meet demand.

India appeared as a new gasoline supplier at 33 kbd in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week, suggesting Indian refiners (running on Russian crude) are redirecting product exports to Japan — a new crisis-driven trade flow.
yearly: Japan's gasoline imports are exhibiting extreme volatility: 73 kbd in H2 2025 → 39 kbd in March (refiners still producing from existing crude stocks) → 92 kbd in the April partial as crude inventories deplete and domestic production collapses.

South Korea (39 kbd in March) and India (emerging in April at 46 kbd) are filling the gap, but Japan's inability to self-supply refined products at current crude import levels creates a cascading dependency on regional refiners who face their own feedstock constraints.

recent: Japan's naphtha imports crashed from 289 kbd (9-15 Mar) to 86 kbd (16-22 Mar) -- a single Qatari cargo -- before partially recovering to 187 kbd (23-29 Mar) on UAE supply (126 kbd); running at 137 kbd in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr as US (50 kbd) and Indian (36 kbd) cargoes arrived.

The Gulf still provided 37-67% of naphtha in W13-W14 (mostly UAE via Fujairah/non-Hormuz routes), but the volatility is extreme -- Bloomberg reports naphtha shortages threatening Japanese cracker operations, with Mitsubishi Chemical cutting Ibaraki output to avoid total shutdown.

Kuwait (70 kbd in W11) and Qatar (86 kbd one-off in W12) have effectively disappeared, replaced by a patchwork of Peru (37 kbd), South Korea (24 kbd), and US cargoes at a massive cost premium with naphtha above $1,000/mt.
quarterly: Japan's naphtha imports showed extreme volatility across Q1, swinging from 392 kbd (W08) to just 86 kbd (W12) as Gulf-sourced cargoes became unreliable. Gulf share averaged 65–84% pre-crisis but hit 37% by the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week.

UAE remained the largest Gulf naphtha supplier (51–126 kbd in post-crisis weeks) alongside Qatar and Kuwait, but the US (45–50 kbd), Peru (37 kbd), India (29–36 kbd), and South Korea emerged as critical non-Gulf alternatives. C&F Japan naphtha jumped from $623 to $756/mt in early March, per Alkagesta.

Japanese petrochemical crackers have been forced to curtail output with naphtha shortfalls, and about 5% of global ethylene capacity was shut in Japan, South Korea, and China by March due to feedstock constraints.
yearly: Japan's naphtha imports — the petrochemical industry's lifeblood — have suffered the most devastating structural decline: from 400 kbd in H2 2025 to 189 kbd in March and 129 kbd in the April partial, a 68% collapse that has forced Japanese producers to curb ethylene output.

Gulf naphtha (75.2% of pre-crisis supply from UAE, Qatar, Kuwait) provided ~300 kbd; by March this had halved to 136 kbd, and April shows zero Gulf naphtha arriving — the supply simply does not exist with Gulf NGL processing shut down.

The US (now entering as a supplier for the first time) and India are filling barely a third of the gap; with naphtha surging past $1,000/mt globally, Japan faces structural deindustrialization of its petrochemical sector unless Hormuz reopens or costly emergency naphtha-for-crude swaps can be arranged.

recent: South Korea's crude imports suffered the most dramatic collapse of any tracked importer: from 3.2 mb/d (9-15 Mar) to just 917 kbd (30 Mar-4 Apr) -- a 71% decline that triggered the government's record 22.46M-barrel SPR release and elevation of the crude oil crisis alert to 'Caution' on Apr 2.

The US surged to become South Korea's largest crude supplier at 497-899 kbd, with Gulf share plunging from 76% to 27% -- but even US barrels couldn't prevent the supply cliff, as Iraq (FM on all foreign-operated fields), Kuwait (Al-Ahmadi drone strikes), and Saudi volumes all cratered.

Mexico appeared as a new supplier (177 kbd) in 30 Mar-4 Apr alongside UAE (243 kbd), while Algeria (154 kbd) and Canada (83 kbd) provided one-off cargoes in W13 -- the desperate diversification underscores why Seoul imposed a naphtha export ban on Mar 27 and Lotte Yeosu halted operations.
quarterly: South Korea's crude imports collapsed from a pre-crisis average of ~2.8 mb/d (Jan–Feb) to just 1.41 mb/d in 23–29 Mar and 0.92 mb/d in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week — a 67% contraction that triggered the government's elevation to "Caution" crisis alert and a record 22.46M bbl SPR release.

The US emerged as the critical alternative supplier, surging from 600–710 kbd pre-crisis to 899 kbd (#1 supplier) in 16–22 Mar and 497 kbd in W14. Gulf share plummeted from 60–76% in January to 26.5% in the partial week, with Saudi Arabia falling from ~1.1 mb/d to 230 kbd.

Diversification expanded dramatically: Algeria, Mexico, Canada, Australia, Ecuador, and even Republic of Congo appeared in the supply mix. Iraq dropped to 136–200 kbd from 274–668 kbd pre-crisis after the FM declaration. Lotte Yeosu refinery halted, and the naphtha export ban since Mar 27 reflects acute feedstock concern.
yearly: South Korea's crude imports cratered from the 2.81 mb/d H2 2025 baseline to 2.40 mb/d in March and a devastating 879 kbd in the April partial — a 69% collapse that triggered Seoul's record SPR release of 22.46 million barrels and elevation to 'Caution' crisis alert.

The US emerged as the crisis lifeline, surging to 579 kbd in March (vs near zero historically), but Gulf barrels still provided 61% of March volumes — meaning the remaining Gulf trickle (mainly Saudi Yanbu and UAE ADCOP bypass) is absolutely critical.

Unlike China and India, South Korea's Russia pivot is negligible (zero Russian crude in March) due to sanctions compliance and refinery configuration for heavier ME grades — leaving Seoul more exposed than any other major Asian importer.

Lotte Yeosu refinery has halted operations, South Korea imposed a naphtha export ban since March 27, and stricter vehicle restrictions are in force — the country is in managed energy rationing mode with inventories up ~13% MoM only due to SPR drawdowns.

recent: South Korea's LNG imports fell from 200 kt/d (9-15 Mar) to 107-117 kt/d (23 Mar-4 Apr), with Qatar vanishing after W11 (16.7 kt/d); Australia remained the anchor at 28-40 kt/d, with Malaysia (20-34 kt/d), Canada, Russia, and even Chinese re-exports (8-21 kt/d) diversifying supply.

KOGAS's claim that inventories exceed mandatory reserves and Qatar represents only 14% of 2026 imports appears validated by the data -- South Korea's pre-crisis long-term contract diversification (Woodside 0.5 Mtpa, TotalEnergies 1 Mtpa deals) is paying dividends, but total volumes are still down ~42% from early March.
quarterly: South Korea's LNG supply weathered the crisis better than most Asian importers, with Qatar representing only ~14% of 2026 imports. Total volumes dipped modestly from ~150–200 ktons/d in Jan–Feb to 107–117 ktons/d in late March, and Gulf share fell to 0% by 23–29 Mar.

Australia remained the anchor (28–68 ktons/d), complemented by Malaysia (17–38 ktons/d), Canada (10–37 ktons/d), Indonesia, Peru, Russia, and China re-exports. The new Woodside 0.5 Mtpa contract and incoming TotalEnergies 1 Mtpa deal provide long-term insulation.

KOGAS reports inventory exceeds mandatory reserves, and the government has downplayed supply concerns. But the doubled JKM spot price ($20.39/MMBtu) imposes a heavy cost on Korea's gas-dependent semiconductor and petrochemical sectors.
yearly: South Korea's LNG supply has held up well: 137 ktons/d in March and 129 ktons/d in April vs the 130 ktons/d H2 2025 baseline — KOGAS's statement that reserves exceed mandatory minimums is consistent with the data showing Qatar's share was already low at ~14%.

Australia (45 ktons/d) and Malaysia (25 ktons/d) dominate, with new long-term deals (Woodside 0.5 Mtpa, TotalEnergies 1 Mtpa) providing forward supply security; however, spot procurement costs have doubled and Gulf LNG has dropped to 0% in the April partial.

The structural takeaway: South Korea's post-Fukushima LNG diversification strategy — deliberately reducing Qatar dependence below 15% — is paying off as the one bright spot in an otherwise devastating energy supply picture.

recent: South Korea has completed a 100% pivot to North American LPG: US (7.6-18.7 kt/d) and Canada (1.7-2.0 kt/d) were the only suppliers across all four weeks, with zero Gulf barrels; volumes dipped to 9.4 kt/d (23-29 Mar) before recovering to 20.7 kt/d in the partial 30 Mar-4 Apr week.

The complete Gulf exit aligns with the shutdown of Saudi/UAE/Qatari NGL processing -- as with Japan, this US-centric LPG supply structure may prove durable given the LPG-naphtha spread favouring Gulf Coast exports.
quarterly: Like Japan, South Korea had fully pivoted LPG procurement to the US and Canada before the crisis, with 100% of imports from North America throughout Q1 2026. Zero Gulf LPG exposure means the ME supply collapse had no direct impact on sourcing.

Volumes declined modestly from 15–31 ktons/d in January to 9–21 ktons/d in late March, but this appears to be demand-driven (cracker curtailments, naphtha-LPG substitution constraints) rather than supply-driven.
yearly: South Korea's LPG imports have fully pivoted to US and Canadian supply (100% share since the crisis began), with volumes declining modestly from 18 ktons/d pre-crisis to 16 ktons/d in March — manageable given the ME LPG share was already negligible at <2% in H2 2025.

The larger LPG impact is indirect: South Korea's naphtha export ban since March 27 means petrochemical operators who might switch to LPG feedstock face both feedstock constraints, as the LPG-naphtha spread has compressed with both commodities under Gulf-linked stress.

recent: Diesel imports were erratic at 0-65 kbd, primarily from Japan (50 kbd spike in 16-22 Mar) with Russia (7.5 kbd) and China (3-13 kbd) appearing in W12-W13; the drop to zero in the partial week 30 Mar-4 Apr likely reflects refinery yield optimisation and inventory drawdowns rather than true demand destruction.
quarterly: South Korea's diesel imports are small and sourced exclusively from regional suppliers (Japan, Russia, China), with zero Gulf dependency. Volumes were highly sporadic, ranging from 0 to 65 kbd, with Russia appearing in some weeks. No meaningful Hormuz exposure.
yearly: South Korea's diesel imports collapsed to zero in the April partial from a baseline of ~28 kbd in H2 2025 — sourced exclusively from Japan, China, and Russia with zero Gulf exposure, so the decline reflects demand destruction and refinery conservation rather than supply disruption.

With Korean refiners cutting crude runs and the government imposing product export restrictions, regional diesel trade is being rationed; Japan's reciprocal need for Korean diesel and gasoline creates a fragile intra-Asian product-swapping ecosystem.

recent: Gasoline imports were intermittent and declining: 108 kbd from Japan (9-15 Mar), then just 24 kbd from Singapore (16-22 Mar), zero (23-29 Mar), and a small 13 kbd Japanese cargo (30 Mar-4 Apr) -- South Korea's refinery complex is maintaining enough domestic gasoline output despite crude supply cuts, aided by the government's first fuel price cap since the Gulf War.
quarterly: South Korea's gasoline imports — small volumes sourced from Japan, Taiwan, and Singapore — showed a notable crisis-driven spike in early March (76–108 kbd in W10–W11) as domestic refinery runs were cut due to crude shortages, before tapering back. Singapore appeared as a new source in W12.
yearly: Gasoline imports spiked 57% to 47 kbd in March — predominantly from Japan (42 kbd) — as domestic refinery run cuts forced South Korea to import finished products, inverting its traditional role as a net refined product exporter.

This role reversal is structurally significant: South Korea's refining sector (3.2 mb/d capacity) was built to process Gulf crude and export products to Asia — with crude at 879 kbd in April, the country is becoming a net product importer for the first time in decades.

recent: Naphtha supply underwent a structural transformation: Gulf share crashed from 96% (9-15 Mar, led by UAE 230 and Qatar 203 kbd) to 0% in the partial 30 Mar-4 Apr week, with total volumes falling from 522 to 399 kbd -- ICIS reports South Korea's petrochemical industry entered 'conservation mode' as YNCC declared FM on ethylene production.

India emerged as the leading replacement supplier at 141 kbd (W12 and W14), alongside a rotating cast of Algeria (42-74 kbd), Greece (21-64 kbd), Peru (49 kbd), and Oman (64-70 kbd) -- the pivot from Gulf to Mediterranean/South Asian naphtha is unprecedented and comes at a massive cost premium, with S. Korea banning naphtha exports since Mar 27 to preserve domestic cracker feedstock.

The supplier volatility (zero repeat supplier mix across consecutive weeks) signals a spot-driven scramble rather than structured procurement, consistent with LG Chem halting its Yeosu naphtha cracker and operating rates industry-wide falling to ~60%.
quarterly: South Korea's naphtha imports — critical feedstock for its petrochemical sector — collapsed from 513–884 kbd in January–February to 335 kbd in 23–29 Mar, with Gulf share plummeting from 52–76% to 31%. By the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week, Gulf share hit 0% for the first time.

The supplier base was completely restructured: India surged to 141 kbd (#1 in W14), Algeria (42–74 kbd), Greece (21–64 kbd), Peru, Egypt, Libya, and Russia all appeared as emergency non-Gulf sources. This aligns with YNCC's FM on ethylene and the Mar 27 naphtha export ban.

The quarterly trajectory is stark — from UAE+Qatar+Bahrain dominating at 370–559 kbd daily in January to zero Gulf naphtha by early April. Crackers have cut to ~60% utilization per ICIS, and Formosa FPCC in Taiwan is similarly at ~50%, reflecting the cascading regional feedstock crisis.
yearly: South Korea's naphtha imports — the backbone of its massive petrochemical industry — fell from 608 kbd in H2 2025 to 513 kbd in March and 428 kbd in the April partial, a 30% decline that has already triggered YNCC's FM on ethylene production.

Gulf naphtha (UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman) provided ~56% of pre-crisis supply at ~340 kbd; by March this held at 302 kbd thanks to pre-loaded cargoes, but April shows zero Gulf naphtha — India (48 kbd), Malaysia, and US are scrambling to partially fill the gap.

The naphtha export ban since March 27 is preserving domestic supply for petrochemical use, but with Formosa Plastics (Taiwan) also declaring FM and Asian naphtha prices past $1,000/mt, the entire Northeast Asian petrochemical supply chain faces structural disruption through at least Q2 2026.

recent: Thai crude imports declined steadily from ~1.25 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~0.63 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr, a 50% contraction as the Hormuz closure increasingly chokes Gulf-origin cargoes that still accounted for 88% of supply in 23–29 Mar.

UAE crude dominated mid-March at ~713 kbd (23–29 Mar) but collapsed to just 60 kbd in the partial week as available Hormuz-transit barrels dried up; Bangkok’s bilateral transit deal with Iran (signed 28 Mar per Nation Thailand) has yet to produce visible cargo flow.

Diversification is scrambling: Indonesia, Brunei, and Nigeria appeared as new suppliers in 30 Mar–4 Apr, but at thin volumes (166, 82, 70 kbd respectively) — nowhere near replacing the ~700 kbd UAE dependency. Deputy PM Phiphat confirmed Thailand is opening Russian crude talks to bridge the shortfall.
quarterly: Thailand's crude imports averaged ~0.9 mb/d across Q1 2026, but the supplier mix transformed: Gulf share swung from 90% (19–25 Jan) to just 29% (9–15 Mar) as US barrels surged to 449 kbd/d and Brazil (159 kbd/d), Australia, and Libya filled gaps — before UAE volumes snapped back to 692–713 kbd/d in the final two weeks of March.

Bangkok secured a bilateral Hormuz transit deal with Iran on Mar 28, joining India, Pakistan, and Malaysia in 'friendly nation' passage — explaining why UAE crude continued flowing even as other importers lost access; Thai Deputy PM also opened talks on purchasing Russian crude to offset remaining shortfalls.

The quarterly trajectory reveals Thailand's structural vulnerability: ~50% Gulf-dependent pre-crisis, the country could diversify short-term but remains hostage to whether Iran's bilateral transit deals hold; the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr showed a sharp pullback to 625 kbd/d with Saudi and Indonesia replacing UAE as top suppliers.
yearly: Thailand's crude imports proved surprisingly resilient through the crisis, averaging 927 kbd in March 2026 vs an 857 kbd 2025 average, as Bangkok's Mar 28 bilateral Hormuz transit deal with Iran temporarily preserved UAE supply at a record 495 kbd — but the 4-day April partial shows UAE vanishing entirely and daily rates dropping to 847 kbd.

The supplier mix underwent a structural shift in early April: Saudi Arabia surged to 315 kbd (#1 for first time), Indonesia jumped to 249 kbd (vs 42 kbd in March), and Nigeria/Brunei appeared — signaling emergency diversification away from UAE-via-Hormuz dependence that supplied 55-71% of Thailand's crude in H2 2025.

Thailand's Deputy PM confirmed plans to open Russian crude negotiations, and the government secured 2M bbl from Angola and 600k bbl from the US, but replacing ~350 kbd of UAE crude (Thailand's 2025 average from UAE alone) requires locking in Atlantic Basin and Russian long-haul contracts at vastly higher freight costs.

recent: Thailand’s LNG imports stabilised at ~40–42 ktons/d through 16 Mar–4 Apr after a softer 25 ktons/d in 9–15 Mar, with Australia anchoring supply at 20–41 ktons/d across all four weeks — the single most reliable source as Qatar’s 77 Mtpa remains entirely offline.

Supply origins rotated aggressively: Mozambique and Canada appeared in 16–22 Mar, Oman bridged in 23–29 Mar (10 ktons/d), and US LNG arrived at 24 ktons/d in the partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr — reflecting spot market competition as Asian JKM surged past $20/MMBtu.

Zero Gulf LNG in 3 of 4 weeks (sole exception: one Oman cargo in 23–29 Mar) underscores complete QatarEnergy FM impact; Thailand’s declining Gulf of Thailand domestic gas production makes securing sustained LNG contracts critical.
quarterly: Thailand's LNG imports held up remarkably well across Q1 — averaging ~35–45 ktons/d — as the country rapidly pivoted from Qatar (which supplied 13–17 ktons/d pre-crisis) to Australia, Mozambique, Canada, and US cargoes; Qatar's last cargo arrived in the 2–8 Mar window before QatarEnergy's FM took full effect.

Australia emerged as Thailand's LNG anchor supplier by late March, delivering 20–41 ktons/d in weeks where Gulf supply was zero, alongside smaller cargoes from China (re-exports) and Oman; the diversified base insulated Thailand better than its crude supply chain.

With declining domestic Gulf of Thailand gas production and Myanmar pipeline uncertainty, Thailand's growing LNG import dependency makes the loss of Qatar (~40% of Gulf LNG share in early January) a structural concern — spot prices well above pre-crisis levels are straining utility budgets even with volumes intact.
yearly: Thailand's LNG imports surged to a 12-month high of 42.4 ktons/day in March 2026, driven by Australia tripling to 15.2 ktons/day and China re-exporting 7.1 ktons/day — compensating for declining Gulf of Thailand domestic gas output that makes the country increasingly import-dependent.

Qatar LNG (6-8 ktons/day through 2025, representing 22-44% of supply) persisted at 6.2 ktons/day through March but dropped to zero in the 4-day April partial, where only US (18 ktons/day) and Australia (9.5 ktons/day) remain — a complete Gulf-to-Pacific supplier pivot.

The April daily rate of 27.5 ktons/day is a sharp pullback from March's 42.4 ktons/day, likely reflecting spot cargo availability constraints as Asian LNG buyers compete for a shrinking non-Qatar pool; Thailand's growing structural gas import dependency leaves it acutely exposed to JKM spot premiums now 2x pre-crisis levels.

recent: Thai diesel imports are low-volume (~8 kbd baseline) with a one-off spike to 45.5 kbd in 16–22 Mar from a single Indian cargo — likely an opportunistic spot purchase as Indian refiners redirected product amid their own refinery run cuts (MRPL FM on gasoline exports).

Suppliers shifted from Malaysia (sole provider in W11/W13 at ~8 kbd) to China and South Korea in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr (3.4 kbd each), signalling ongoing sourcing instability and negligible Gulf exposure.
quarterly: Thailand's diesel imports are minimal (~1–9 kbd/d typically) and entirely sourced from regional refiners — Malaysia, Singapore, China, South Korea — with zero Gulf dependency, limiting direct crisis exposure.

A notable spike to 46 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar from a single Indian cargo suggests Thailand is tapping new suppliers as regional product markets tighten; the 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr showed China and South Korea splitting supply at 7 kbd/d combined.
yearly: Thailand's diesel imports exploded from near-zero (0-2 kbd throughout 2025) to 15.4 kbd in March 2026, with India emerging as the primary emergency supplier at 10.3 kbd — a direct consequence of Thai refineries cutting throughput as Gulf crude supply tightened.

The April partial (10.1 kbd from China and South Korea) confirms this is a structural shift, not a one-off: as Thailand's refinery utilization drops alongside crude availability, the country is transitioning from crude importer to refined product importer, a significantly more expensive and less flexible supply chain.

recent: Thai gasoline imports surged nearly 5x from 5.4 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 24.7 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr, with Malaysia joining Singapore as supplier in 23–29 Mar and South Korea appearing in the latest week — a clear sign domestic refinery runs are being curtailed as crude feedstock tightens.

Entirely sourced from regional Asian hubs (Singapore, Malaysia, South Korea) with zero Gulf exposure — the product supply chain is functioning, but the growing import bill at crisis-era prices compounds Thailand’s $45B+ annual oil import cost.
quarterly: Thailand's gasoline imports are negligible (typically 0–9 kbd/d), sourced entirely from Singapore, South Korea, and Malaysia — reflecting strong domestic refining capacity rather than self-sufficiency; the crisis impact is indirect via higher crude input costs.

A modest uptick to 16–25 kbd/d in late March (23 Mar–4 Apr) from Malaysia and Singapore may signal domestic refiners throttling back as crude feedstock costs surge, forcing marginal product imports to meet demand.
yearly: Gasoline imports surged to 12.4 kbd in March 2026 (from a 2025 average of ~3 kbd), with Singapore supplying 8.1 kbd and Malaysia 3.7 kbd — reflecting the same refinery throughput cuts driving diesel import spikes.

Southeast Asia's fuel crisis is deepening: the Thai government imposed travel restrictions in March as domestic refining output declined, and the 6.3 kbd April partial rate suggests continued product import dependence until Gulf crude flows normalize.

recent: Thai naphtha imports effectively collapsed: a single 37 kbd Bahrain cargo arrived 9–15 Mar (likely loaded before Bapco Sitra’s destruction), followed by zero in 16–22 Mar, a token 3.2 kbd Singapore cargo in 23–29 Mar, and zero again in the partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr.

With naphtha surging past $1,000/mt globally and Thailand’s petrochemical sector 100% dependent on Gulf naphtha, the supply vacuum directly threatens downstream crackers — mirroring Taiwan’s Formosa crisis and aligning with Chandra Asri (Indonesia) and YNCC (South Korea) FM declarations on naphtha-dependent operations.
quarterly: Thailand's naphtha supply chain has effectively collapsed: imports plunged from a pre-crisis average of ~85–180 kbd/d (100% Gulf-sourced from Saudi Arabia and Kuwait) to ZERO in 16–22 Mar and again in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr, with only a 3.2 kbd/d trickle from Singapore in 23–29 Mar.

The quarterly arc is devastating — Saudi and Kuwait supplied every barrel of Thailand's naphtha through mid-February, then Bahrain briefly substituted (72 kbd/d in 2–8 Mar, 37 kbd/d in 9–15 Mar) before Bapco's Sitra refinery was struck again and that source also vanished.

Thai petrochemical crackers face acute feedstock starvation; naphtha prices surged past $1,000/mt globally, and with no non-Gulf alternative established after 13 weeks, Thailand's petchem sector is operating blind — the bilateral Hormuz transit deal has not yet translated into resumed naphtha flows.
yearly: Thailand's naphtha supply collapsed from 91 kbd in January 2026 (100% Gulf-sourced, primarily Saudi Arabia and Kuwait) to just 34.7 kbd in March and zero in the April partial — a catastrophic disruption given nearly 100% Middle East dependence throughout 2025.

The March supply consisted almost entirely of Bahrain-origin naphtha (24.5 kbd) plus a small US cargo (9.5 kbd), but with Bapco Sitra destroyed and the Strait closed, the pipeline has effectively shut off; Thailand's petrochemical crackers face an existential feedstock crisis with no realistic alternative supply at current volumes.

recent: Vietnam’s crude imports collapsed from ~286 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% Kuwait) to zero for two consecutive weeks (23 Mar–4 Apr), with only a single 88 kbd Nigerian cargo in 16–22 Mar bridging the gap — a supply crisis given Nghi Son Refinery’s ~80% Kuwait dependency.

PM Chinh’s direct calls to Kuwait’s PM and his March 23 Moscow visit (per The Diplomat) have yet to produce visible crude arrivals; Decree 72/2026 cutting MFN tariffs on petroleum products to 0% (effective 9 Mar) signals Hanoi is pivoting to product imports as a stopgap while crude sourcing negotiations continue.

At zero crude inflow, Nghi Son and Binh Son refineries face imminent throughput collapse — Vietnam has asked Japan and South Korea for emergency crude access (per Argus), but competition for non-Gulf barrels is fierce across all Asian importers.
quarterly: Vietnam's crude supply chain suffered a catastrophic break: imports ran at a steady ~286 kbd/d through mid-March — almost entirely from Kuwait — before collapsing to just 88 kbd/d (a single Nigerian cargo) in 16–22 Mar, then ZERO for the final two weeks of the quarter.

Kuwait supplied ~80% of Vietnam's crude imports pre-crisis via long-term contracts feeding Nghi Son refinery; KPC's FM declaration (Mar 7) and subsequent infrastructure devastation — including drone strikes on Al-Ahmadi and the KPC HQ attack on Apr 5 — have severed this lifeline with no clear timeline for restoration.

PM Chinh's Mar 23 visit to Moscow and Decree 72/2026 (cutting tariffs on petrol/diesel/naphtha to 0%) signal desperation-mode diversification, but Russian crude has not yet appeared in Vietnam's seaborne import data; the country is burning through strategic reserves while Nghi Son scrambles for US and West African barrels per Argus Media.
yearly: Vietnam's crude imports collapsed from 351 kbd in February 2026 to 149 kbd in March and zero in April — the most severe supply disruption of any country in this batch, driven by near-total dependence on Kuwait (averaging 230 kbd or ~80% of imports throughout 2025).

Kuwait's FM declaration on Mar 7 and production collapse to ~500 kbd (from 2.6M baseline) devastated Vietnam's supply chain: the KPC Shuwaikh HQ drone strikes on Apr 5 and production at 81% below baseline make near-term recovery impossible.

Nghi Son Refinery is scrambling for US and Qatari crudes while PM Chinh visited Moscow in March to negotiate Russian supply, but replacing 250+ kbd of Kuwaiti crude requires entirely new supply chains; Vietnam's Mar 9 emergency tariff cut (Decree 72/2026: MFN to 0% on fuels through April) signals the severity of the crisis.

recent: Vietnam’s jet fuel imports ran at 17–27 kbd through 9–29 Mar, sourced exclusively from China and Singapore, but dropped to zero in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr — a potential signal of refinery product tightening cascading through the regional jet fuel market.
Zero Gulf exposure throughout; China’s role as sole jet supplier in W11–W12 (~18–20 kbd) reflects Vietnam’s growing dependency on Chinese refined product exports as domestic refining collapses.
quarterly: Vietnam's jet fuel imports trended lower across Q1, falling from ~27–43 kbd/d in January to ~17–27 kbd/d in March, with China and Singapore as the only consistent suppliers after a one-off Saudi cargo (78 kbd/d) in 23 Feb–1 Mar — the last Gulf-linked barrel in the dataset.

The 6-day partial 30 Mar–4 Apr registered ZERO jet fuel imports — a concerning signal as Southeast Asian governments impose travel restrictions and airlines cut capacity; domestic refining at Nghi Son is severely curtailed with no Kuwait crude arriving since mid-March.
yearly: Vietnam's jet fuel imports run 22-34 kbd primarily from China and Singapore (zero Gulf dependence in 2025), providing relative insulation — though the unusual February spike to 50.7 kbd with Saudi Arabia contributing 19.4 kbd suggests a brief arbitrage opportunity before Hormuz fully closed.

March imports dropped to 22.1 kbd and April shows zero, likely reflecting both seasonal airline demand reduction and tightening availability as regional refineries cut throughput amid the feedstock crisis.

recent: Vietnam’s diesel imports nearly tripled from 77 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 218 kbd (23–29 Mar) as the country compensates for collapsing domestic refinery output — South Korea supplies ~70–80% of volumes, effectively becoming Vietnam’s emergency diesel lifeline at 100–163 kbd.

India appeared as a new diesel supplier in the partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr at 47 kbd, and Taiwan sent a 48 kbd cargo in 23–29 Mar — both crisis-era trade flows that didn’t exist pre-conflict, reflecting the scramble for product barrels across Asia.
Running at ~147 kbd in the 6-day partial week; if sustained, this implies Vietnam is importing ~4.4 mb/month of diesel alone, a massive balance-of-payments burden at $110+ Brent.
quarterly: South Korea has become Vietnam's diesel lifeline: supplying 38–163 kbd/d consistently across Q1, South Korean refiners maintained or even increased diesel flows in the crisis period — 163 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar and 158 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar — as Vietnam's domestic refining output collapsed.

Diesel imports actually rose in late March (218 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar, the quarter's highest) with Taiwan (48 kbd/d) and India (47 kbd/d in the partial week) emerging as new suppliers, reflecting the scramble for refined products as Nghi Son's crude supply evaporated.

Vietnam's zero Gulf dependency for diesel (100% sourced from regional refiners) is a rare bright spot, but the cost premium is severe — Decree 72/2026's tariff cuts signal Hanoi is subsidizing fuel affordability as the manufacturing sector squeezes.
yearly: Vietnam's diesel imports surged to a 12-month high of 205 kbd in the 4-day April partial (vs a 2025 average of ~89 kbd), with South Korea at 136 kbd and India emerging at 70 kbd — more than doubling normal volumes as Nghi Son Refinery cuts runs due to Kuwait crude loss.

South Korea has been Vietnam's dominant diesel supplier throughout 2025 (35-106 kbd), and the April surge suggests Seoul's refiners are redirecting product exports to Southeast Asia where margins are highest; Vietnam's zero-tariff emergency decree is further incentivizing this flow.

recent: Singapore is Vietnam’s sole gasoline lifeline, supplying 17–30 kbd across all four weeks; volumes declined from a peak of 43 kbd (9–15 Mar, when Japan also contributed 14 kbd) to ~20 kbd in the partial week, consistent with Singapore Refining Co. cutting its own runs to 60%.

Vietnam’s emergency 0% tariff on gasoline imports (Decree 72, effective 9 Mar) has not prevented a volume decline — the constraint is physical supply, not economics, as Asian refining capacity contracts across the board.
quarterly: Vietnam's gasoline imports (typically 17–64 kbd/d) are sourced entirely from Singapore and Thailand, with no Gulf dependency; volumes declined to ~17–23 kbd/d in March from pre-crisis highs as Southeast Asian governments imposed fuel conservation measures.
