The appearance of a Japanese cargo (14 kbd/d) in 9–15 Mar was a first for Vietnam's gasoline supply — signalling that traditional trade flows are being disrupted and new routes forming as the regional product market reshuffles.
yearly: Gasoline imports rose to 33 kbd in March 2026 (vs a 22 kbd 2025 average), entirely from Singapore (19.6 kbd) and Malaysia (10.4 kbd), as domestic refinery output declined alongside crude supply — Singapore's status as ASEAN's product balancing hub is being tested by simultaneous demand from multiple crisis-hit buyers.
The April partial at 29.6 kbd (100% Singapore) confirms sustained elevated demand; Vietnam's Decree 72/2026 tariff cuts are designed precisely to facilitate these emergency product flows.

recent: Zero naphtha imports for three weeks (9–29 Mar), then a 56 kbd Russian cargo appeared in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr — the first tangible result of PM Chinh’s March Moscow visit and the US Treasury sanctions waiver (12 Mar–11 Apr) enabling Russian petroleum product deliveries.

If sustained, Russian naphtha could partially offset Vietnam’s petrochemical feedstock crisis, but the sanctions waiver expires April 11 and volumes remain a fraction of Asia’s ~3 mb/d naphtha import need.
quarterly: Vietnam's naphtha imports were sporadic even pre-crisis (only Saudi Arabia and India appearing intermittently at ~72–78 kbd/d), and the supply went entirely dark from 2 Mar through 29 Mar — five consecutive weeks of zero imports.

A Russian Federation naphtha cargo of 56 kbd/d arrived in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week — the first Russian naphtha in Vietnam's dataset and a direct outcome of PM Chinh's Moscow energy agreements; this is a tangible shift, though volumes remain far below what's needed to restart naphtha-dependent petrochemical units.
yearly: Vietnam's naphtha imports were sporadic throughout 2025 (0-48 kbd, Gulf-sourced when present) and collapsed to 10.9 kbd in March (100% Russian Federation) then zero in April — a minor volume but indicative of how completely Gulf-origin petrochemical feedstocks have been cut off across Southeast Asia.

recent: US crude imports declined from ~3.2 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to ~1.9 mb/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr, but the US as the world’s largest crude producer (~13 mb/d) and net exporter is not supply-constrained — the drop reflects cargo scheduling variability, not structural shortage.

Venezuela emerged as a top-3 supplier at 234–493 kbd across all four weeks, enabled by the US Treasury sanctions waiver (12 Mar–11 Apr); Saudi Arabia held steady at ~344–494 kbd via Yanbu pipeline bypass. Gulf share fluctuated between 18–28% — well within normal range.

Supplier diversification is extraordinary: 13+ origins each week including Mexico, Canada, Guyana, Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, Ecuador, Panama, Ghana, UK, Egypt, and Libya — the most diversified crude import slate of any major economy, insulating US refiners from single-source dependency.
quarterly: US crude imports averaged ~2.6 mb/d across Q1 2026, barely disrupted by the Hormuz crisis thanks to Western Hemisphere dominance: Canada, Mexico, Venezuela, Colombia, and Brazil collectively supplied 60–75% of barrels, with Gulf crude (Saudi via Yanbu, Iraq via Ceyhan) adding a manageable 15–28% — all bypassing Hormuz entirely.

The quarterly high of 3.2 mb/d (9–15 Mar) coincided with Mexico surging to 634 kbd/d and Saudi maintaining 589 kbd/d via Red Sea loading, demonstrating that US refiners were actively pulling barrels from every available Atlantic Basin and pipeline-connected source.

Venezuela's resurgence is striking: from 127–265 kbd/d in January to 403–493 kbd/d in March, Venezuelan crude now rivals Saudi Arabia as a top-3 US import source — enabled by the Mar 12–Apr 11 sanctions waiver as Washington prioritized supply security over sanctions enforcement.
yearly: US crude imports held steady at ~2,650 kbd in Feb-March 2026 (vs a 2025 average of 2,475 kbd), with Saudi Arabia surging to the #1 supplier at 431-548 kbd — up from a 260 kbd 2025 average — as Yanbu pipeline volumes bypass the Hormuz closure and reach the US via Red Sea/Atlantic routing.

Venezuela emerged as a major new source at 424 kbd in March (from near-zero pre-crisis), enabled by the Mar 12 US sanctions waiver, while traditional Western Hemisphere suppliers (Canada 221 kbd, Mexico 330 kbd) continued steady flows — the US refinery system's flexibility in processing diverse crude slates is a structural advantage.

Gulf share is climbing (17-21% in 2025 to 23-27% in Q1 2026), which appears counterintuitive during a Hormuz crisis but reflects Saudi Arabia's Yanbu pipeline bypass making its crude more competitive vs alternatives; the risk is growing concentration on a single chokepoint bypass.

recent: South Korea dominates US jet fuel imports at 60–137 kbd across all four weeks, with Canada providing a steady 24–35 kbd backup. Taiwan appeared briefly at 10 kbd in 23–29 Mar as FPCC’s run cuts freed exportable product.
Zero Gulf jet fuel exposure throughout — the US jet supply chain runs entirely through Pacific and North American sources, with volumes stabilising at ~120–141 kbd in 23 Mar–4 Apr after a W12 dip.
quarterly: US jet fuel imports are almost entirely a South Korea–Canada duopoly; South Korean flows have been volatile — collapsing to zero in 2–8 Mar before rebounding to 137 kbd/d the following week — reflecting the knock-on effects of South Korea's naphtha export ban (Mar 27) and refinery run adjustments.

New suppliers emerged in March: China (44 kbd/d in 9–15 Mar) and Taiwan (10 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar) appeared for the first time, as FPCC and other Asian refiners facing cracker shutdowns redirected surplus product to the US market.
yearly: US jet fuel imports declined from a 2025 average of 145 kbd to 118 kbd in March 2026, dominated by South Korea (82 kbd) with zero Gulf sourcing — a structurally resilient supply chain, though the April partial collapse to 50 kbd (Canada only) may signal tightening availability as Asian refiners cut runs and prioritize domestic markets.

recent: Canada supplies ~85–90% of US diesel imports, running at 89–178 kbd across all four weeks; Turkey contributed a one-off 64 kbd cargo in 9–15 Mar and South Korea filled in at 5–14 kbd through 23 Mar–4 Apr.

Volumes normalised at ~107–110 kbd in the most recent two weeks after an elevated 242 kbd in 9–15 Mar; zero Gulf exposure throughout, reflecting the integrated North American diesel market’s structural insulation from Hormuz disruption.
quarterly: US diesel imports are insulated from the Gulf crisis — Canada supplies 60–70% of volumes (~90–180 kbd/d) with Mexico as the secondary source; zero Gulf diesel has entered the US throughout Q1.

Turkey emerged as a new diesel supplier in early March (53–64 kbd/d in W10–W11), likely redirecting product previously bound for Mediterranean markets as European demand patterns shift; this flow tapered by late March as Turkish refiners faced their own crude sourcing challenges.
yearly: US diesel imports are structurally Canada-dependent (90-173 kbd or 80%+ of volume throughout 2025-26) with no meaningful Gulf component, providing near-complete insulation from the Hormuz crisis; Turkey emerged as a new niche supplier in Q1 2026 (23-26 kbd), likely redirecting product from disrupted Middle Eastern markets.
The elevated Jan-Feb volumes (~252 kbd vs a 114 kbd 2025 average) suggest pre-crisis stockpiling, now reverting toward normal: March at 158 kbd, April partial at 81 kbd.

recent: US gasoline imports rebounded to ~432 kbd in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr from a low of 288 kbd in 23–29 Mar, with Canada (177 kbd), South Korea (91 kbd), and Bahamas (84 kbd) leading — the highest daily rate in the 4-week window.

India emerged as a recurring gasoline supplier at 44–49 kbd in 16–29 Mar, a novel crisis-era trade flow as Indian refiners redirect product barrels westward. UK and South Korea also stepped up, reflecting global product-market arbitrage as $110+ Brent reshuffles refinery economics.
quarterly: US gasoline imports averaged ~330 kbd/d across Q1 from an unusually diversified base — Canada, Bahamas (Caribbean refining hub), UK, India, South Korea, and multiple European countries — with zero direct Gulf dependency throughout the quarter.

European gasoline cargoes to the US increased notably: Belgium, Netherlands, Portugal, Spain, and Sweden all appeared as suppliers in the crisis period, consistent with European crackers cutting back on petrochemicals (due to naphtha costs surging $250–300/mt per LyondellBasell's FM) and redirecting light product to Atlantic Basin buyers.
yearly: US gasoline imports remained stable at 334-338 kbd in Feb-March 2026 (vs a 430 kbd 2025 average), sourced overwhelmingly from the Western Hemisphere (Canada, Bahamas, Netherlands) with zero Gulf exposure — the US product market is effectively decoupled from the Hormuz disruption.

The April partial rate of 440 kbd is elevated, with South Korea appearing at 137 kbd for the first time in months, possibly reflecting Korean refiners redirecting surplus gasoline to the US as Asian domestic demand contracts under the fuel crisis.

recent: US naphtha imports shifted from European origins (Belgium, Netherlands, France at combined 67 kbd in 9–15 Mar) to South American and African sources (Angola 49 kbd, Brazil 36 kbd in 23–29 Mar), then dropped to zero in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr.

As the world’s largest ethane-based petrochemical producer, the US faces negligible naphtha supply risk — these small imports (45–96 kbd) serve niche refinery blending, not cracker feedstock, giving US petchem a structural advantage over naphtha-dependent Asian competitors.
quarterly: US naphtha imports flipped from Latin American sources (Spain, Argentina, Israel, Brazil at 20–80 kbd/d) to a European flood in early March — France (50 kbd/d), Belgium (25–31 kbd/d), and Netherlands (21–46 kbd/d) all surged, reflecting European crackers dumping feedstock as they shut down under $1,000/mt naphtha economics.

The US naphtha market is a net exporter, so these import volumes are small (~45–120 kbd/d), but the European origin shift is a leading indicator of how severely the Gulf crisis has disrupted the Atlantic Basin petrochemical chain; the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week showed zero imports.
yearly: US naphtha imports averaged 57 kbd in 2025, sourced entirely from Atlantic Basin suppliers (Netherlands, Belgium, Argentina, Spain) with zero Gulf dependence; March 2026 at 74.5 kbd represents a modest uptick, likely European petrochemical feedstock being redirected as EU crackers cut runs amid elevated naphtha costs per the LyondellBasell FM declaration.

recent: Taiwan’s crude imports plunged from ~864 kbd (9–15 Mar) to just 294 kbd in 23–29 Mar — the US was the ONLY supplier that week at 294 kbd, with Kuwait and Saudi Arabia both gone since mid-March as Hormuz transit collapsed.

Partial recovery to ~663 kbd in the 6-day week 30 Mar–4 Apr as Oman appeared at 314 kbd alongside US crude at 349 kbd — Oman’s 3 vessels transited Hormuz via an alternative coastal route on April 2 (per Hellenic Shipping News), potentially opening a narrow lifeline.

FPCC (Mailiao complex) confirmed further April run cuts per Argus; at ~0.3–0.7 mb/d imports vs pre-crisis ~0.9 mb/d, Taiwan is burning through its 150-day strategic reserves faster than planned. Formosa Plastics declared FM on PVC, HDPE, and polyethylene effective April 1.
quarterly: Taiwan's crude imports followed a dramatic collapse trajectory across Q1: from 1.0–1.5 mb/d in January (68–100% Gulf-sourced from Saudi, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, Oman) to just 294 kbd/d in 23–29 Mar when US crude was the ONLY supplier — a 70–80% decline from peak weeks.

The US has become Taiwan's critical crude lifeline, appearing in 8 of 13 weeks at 294–596 kbd/d; Oman appeared in the partial 30 Mar–4 Apr week (314 kbd/d) — its first crisis-era cargo and a strategically significant development given Oman's export terminals sit outside the Hormuz chokepoint.

With approximately 120–150 days of oil reserves, Taiwan has more runway than its 11-day gas buffer, but the trajectory is alarming: Gulf-sourced crude averaged 700+ kbd/d pre-crisis versus effectively zero in late March, and any summer electricity demand surge could force drawdowns that strain semiconductor manufacturing per the Atlantic Council.
yearly: Taiwan's crude imports deteriorated from 766 kbd in February to 642 kbd in March 2026 (vs a 2025 average of 795 kbd), with the US overtaking Saudi Arabia as #1 supplier at 354 kbd — a historic reorientation for an island that sourced ~70% from the Gulf throughout 2025.

The 4-day April partial shows an alarming narrowing to a single Omani cargo (471 kbd daily rate, 100% Gulf) — extreme concentration risk that underscores Taiwan's difficulty securing diversified supply; Formosa Petrochemical (FPCC) has already slashed runs to ~50% and declared FM on multiple ethylene downstream products as of April 1.

With 150 days of strategic reserves providing a theoretical buffer, the structural challenge is sourcing replacement barrels: Taiwan's 70% ME crude dependence pre-crisis means replacing ~550 kbd of Gulf crude with US, West African, and Latin American barrels at competitive prices — a multi-year procurement transformation now being attempted in weeks.

recent: Qatar LNG vanished after 9–15 Mar (26 ktons/d, 38% of supply that week) following QatarEnergy’s global FM; Australia stepped up as anchor supplier, rising from 22 to 38–41 ktons/d and providing 50%+ of Taiwan’s LNG through late March.

US LNG arrived at 35 ktons/d in the 6-day partial week 30 Mar–4 Apr — the first significant American cargo — while Equatorial Guinea (10.4 ktons/d, 23–29 Mar) and Brunei (9.4–9.9 ktons/d) filled gaps. Gulf share fell from 38% to zero.

Taiwan has assured LNG supply through April, but with 38% pre-crisis Qatar dependence and 99% energy import reliance (per Atlantic Council), the race to lock in spot cargoes at $20+/MMBtu JKM prices is a major fiscal exposure — particularly as the Iran war tests Taiwan’s energy resilience in ways that echo potential future China contingencies.
quarterly: Taiwan executed a rapid LNG supply pivot: Qatar's share fell from 36–54% of imports in January (25–39 ktons/d) to zero by 23–29 Mar, replaced by a surge from Australia (up to 41 ktons/d from a pre-crisis 10–22 ktons/d) and continued US cargoes (~10–35 ktons/d).

Total LNG volumes held up better than expected — averaging ~60–73 ktons/d in March versus 53–75 ktons/d pre-crisis — as Papua New Guinea, Brunei, Equatorial Guinea, and Canada all contributed emergency cargoes; Oman also delivered ~10 ktons/d in W12–W13, bypassing Hormuz from its Sohar facility.

But Taiwan's 11-day natural gas inventory buffer — the thinnest among major East Asian LNG importers per the Atlantic Council — means any supply disruption or summer cooling demand surge could trigger power rationing that cascades through the global semiconductor supply chain; the margin for error is razor-thin even with volumes currently adequate.
yearly: Taiwan's LNG imports proved remarkably resilient in March-April 2026, with daily volumes rising to 65.8 ktons/day in March and 73.1 ktons/day in the April partial — the highest rate of the 12-month period — as US (34.4 ktons/day) and Australian (38.7 ktons/day) cargoes fully replaced Qatar.

Qatar's decline was gradual: from 22-29 ktons/day (35-45% share) through most of 2025, to 9.9 ktons/day in March, to zero in April — the QatarEnergy FM extension to Chinese/Korean contracts in late March has pushed remaining spot cargoes westward, leaving Taiwan to compete aggressively for Pacific Basin supply.

Taiwan's vulnerability lies in summer: electricity demand peaks July-September, natural gas provides ~40% of power generation, and the current 99% LNG import dependence means any disruption to US/Australian supply would trigger immediate power rationing — per the Atlantic Council, this war has exposed Taiwan's 'energy Achilles heel.'

recent: Taiwan’s diesel imports dropped from 97 kbd (9–15 Mar, 100% South Korea) to 18 kbd (23–29 Mar) as FPCC run cuts reduced both domestic demand and product availability; Russia emerged at 50 kbd in 30 Mar–4 Apr, becoming the dominant diesel supplier alongside China at 7 kbd.

The Russia pivot in diesel mirrors broader Asian diversification: the US Treasury sanctions waiver (valid through 11 Apr) enabled Russian product flows into a market scrambling for alternatives to Gulf-origin refined products.
quarterly: Taiwan's diesel imports shifted from a pre-crisis mix of South Korea, China, and India to an increasingly Russian-supplied picture: Russian diesel appeared at 18 kbd/d (9–15 Feb), disappeared, then surged to 50 kbd/d in the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial week alongside China (7 kbd/d).

South Korea remains the swing supplier for Taiwan's diesel (51–97 kbd/d when it appears), but Russia's growing presence reflects the broader Asian scramble for non-Gulf refined products — and Taiwan's willingness to tap sanctioned-adjacent supply chains under crisis conditions.
yearly: Taiwan's diesel imports averaged 50 kbd in 2025 (primarily South Korea), and the April partial surged to 85 kbd with Russian Federation dominating at 74.6 kbd — a dramatic pivot from zero Russian diesel imports in 2025, reflecting emergency procurement as FPCC slashes refinery runs.

South Korea supplied 12-78 kbd throughout 2025, but Russia's sudden emergence as the primary diesel supplier in April signals that Western-aligned Taiwan is pragmatically accepting Russian barrels at crisis prices — a geopolitically sensitive trade-off that may face political scrutiny.

recent: Taiwan imported gasoline only once in the 4-week window — a 25 kbd cargo from Japan in 16–22 Mar — reflecting an emergency spot purchase as FPCC’s Mailiao complex cut runs to ~50%. CPC Corp’s domestic refining has partially offset the FPCC shortfall, limiting product import needs for now.
quarterly: Taiwan's gasoline imports are minimal and sporadic — typically zero most weeks, with occasional cargoes from South Korea (36 kbd/d in mid-January) and Singapore (49 kbd/d in early February); the crisis has exposed FPCC's role as Taiwan's primary domestic gasoline producer, now operating crackers at ~50% capacity.

A single Japanese gasoline cargo (25 kbd/d in 16–22 Mar) appeared as FPCC's Mailiao refinery curtailed operations — if naphtha supply doesn't resume, Taiwan may need sustained product imports that its limited import infrastructure is not designed to handle.
yearly: Taiwan's gasoline imports were sporadic and minimal throughout 2025-26 (0-20 kbd), sourced variably from Singapore, South Korea, Japan, and China — the small volumes and absence of Gulf dependence make this a low-concern commodity in the current crisis.

recent: Taiwan’s naphtha supply suffered the most severe disruption in this batch: 100% Gulf-sourced at 147 kbd (9–15 Mar, UAE + Iraq) fell to 95 kbd (16–22 Mar, UAE only), then dropped to absolute zero for both 23–29 Mar and 30 Mar–4 Apr.

This is the root cause of Formosa Petrochemical’s cascading FM declarations: FPCC crackers at ~50% capacity (since 10 Mar), Formosa Chemicals FM (25 Mar), and Formosa Plastics FM on PVC/HDPE/PE (1 Apr). With naphtha past $1,000/mt and zero non-Gulf alternative suppliers found, further cracker shutdowns are imminent.

Taiwan’s petrochemical crisis has global downstream implications — Formosa is a top-5 global PVC producer, and the supply vacuum is disrupting construction material supply chains worldwide (per CNN/KION).
quarterly: Taiwan's naphtha imports collapsed in the most devastating supply chain failure in this dataset: from a diverse pre-crisis base of 43–197 kbd/d (Russia, UAE, Kuwait, US, Singapore, India) to ZERO in both 23–29 Mar and the 30 Mar–4 Apr partial — directly causing Formosa Petrochemical's force majeure and cracker shutdowns to ~50% capacity.

The quarterly arc reveals how quickly feedstock diversification failed: UAE and Iraq provided 127–152 kbd/d as late as 9–15 Mar, but as naphtha surged past $1,000/mt and Gulf loading became impossible, even non-Gulf suppliers (US, Singapore) could not fill the gap; Formosa Plastics Corp declared FM on PVC, HDPE, and polyethylene effective Apr 1.

This is the single most critical supply chain for Taiwan's economy: naphtha feeds the petrochemical chain that underpins plastics, construction materials (PVC), and packaging across Asia — Formosa's FM means global PVC markets face months of tightened supply, with ripple effects into construction and manufacturing sectors worldwide.
yearly: Taiwan's naphtha supply collapsed to zero in the April partial from a 2025 average of 147 kbd, devastating FPCC's already-halved cracker operations — Russia had been the #1 naphtha supplier (56-118 kbd or 30-50% share throughout 2025), but even Russian flows dried up in April.

UAE was the second-largest naphtha source (11-73 kbd in 2025), and its loss via Hormuz closure compounded by Russia's failure to fill the gap explains Formosa Plastics' April 1 FM declaration on PVC, HDPE, and polyethylene — the entire Mailiao petrochemical complex is at existential risk.

The naphtha shortage ripples globally: Bloomberg reported that the Asia-wide naphtha crunch threatens 'supply chain chaos' in plastics, synthetic textiles, and construction materials, with Taiwan and Japan as the most exposed crackers given their near-total import dependence for this critical feedstock.

recent: UAE gasoline exports were near-zero: a single ~42 kbd cargo in 23–29 Mar with no identified destination, and nothing in all other weeks. The destruction of Ruwais refining and ongoing attacks on UAE energy infrastructure have eliminated the UAE's product export capacity — a complete reversal from the pre-war position as a major gasoline supplier to East Africa and South Asia.
quarterly: UAE gasoline exports followed the same pattern as diesel — collapsing from a pre-crisis average of ~170 kbd (Pakistan as anchor buyer) to effectively zero by mid-March. A tiny 42 kbd cargo appeared in 23–29 Mar with no named destination, suggesting possible ship-to-ship transfer or grey-market activity.
The Fujairah bunkering hub — the world's 2nd largest — is under FM (MEE and Pearl Marine declarations), compounding the refined products shutdown.
yearly: UAE gasoline exports through 2025 served Oman, East African markets (Kenya, Tanzania, Djibouti), and some European destinations at ~120 kbd monthly average. With Ruwais Refinery-2 offline and Habshan gas shut again (Apr 3), the UAE's refinery throughput is severely curtailed.

The Fujairah bunkering hub -- the world's second-largest, where MEE and Pearl Marine have both declared FM -- adds another dimension: UAE's traditional role as a refined products hub for marine fuel, gasoline, and diesel is functionally suspended, redirecting regional fuel logistics toward Oman's Sohar and Singapore.
